locations.name: Deep Creek Hot Springs
locations.description: Deep Creek Hot Springs are among Southern California's most popular backcountry hot springs, with several pools of varying temperature (100–108°F) along the banks of Deep Creek. The hike in is 3–6 miles depending on the trailhead. Located in San Bernardino National Forest, the springs are clothing-optional and draw a diverse crowd. Spring and fall offer the best conditions; flash floods are a hazard in the canyon during storms.
locations.location: San Bernardino National Forest, San Bernardino County

locations.name: Travertine Hot Springs
locations.description: Travertine Hot Springs, just south of Bridgeport, offers stunning views of the Eastern Sierra and multiple tiered pools formed by calcium carbonate deposits that give the springs their name. Temperatures range from a gentle 80°F in upper pools to a scalding 115°F near the source — choose your pool carefully. Managed by the BLM, the site is free and open year-round. The dramatic white travertine formations and Sierra backdrop make this one of California's most photogenic springs.
locations.location: BLM land south of Bridgeport, Mono County

locations.name: Buckeye Hot Springs
locations.description: Buckeye Hot Springs sits in a canyon along Buckeye Creek in the Toiyabe National Forest, with a striking waterfall of hot mineral water cascading into a cool creek below. Soakers can mix hot and cold to find their perfect temperature. The drive in via a dirt road requires high clearance in wet conditions. At roughly 110°F at the source, it's best enjoyed in the pool just above creek level. A true Eastern Sierra gem.
locations.location: Toiyabe National Forest, near Bridgeport, Mono County

locations.name: Wild Willy's Hot Springs
locations.description: Wild Willy's Hot Springs (also called Crowley Hot Springs) are a cluster of shallow soaking pools on BLM land east of Mammoth Lakes with panoramic views of the Eastern Sierra. A short boardwalk leads from the parking area across the meadow to the pools, which range from 100–107°F. Open year-round, though winter access requires a snowmobile or skis. Named for a local character, the site is free and very accessible compared to other Eastern Sierra springs.
locations.location: BLM land east of Mammoth Lakes, Mono County

locations.name: Saline Valley Warm Springs
locations.description: Saline Valley Warm Springs are an oasis in the remote Saline Valley within Death Valley National Park. Three developed pools sit in a palm-studded desert setting at around 105°F. Reaching them requires 4WD or high-clearance vehicle on 50+ miles of rough dirt road — plan for a multi-day trip. A caretaker community maintains the site, which has an established camping area with outhouses. Clothing is optional. This is one of California's most remote and spectacular soaking destinations.
locations.location: Death Valley National Park backcountry, Inyo County

locations.name: Hot Creek Geological Site
locations.description: Hot Creek Geological Site near Mammoth Lakes is a spectacular geothermal area where boiling water erupts from the creek bed, creating vivid blue-green pools surrounded by steam vents. Soaking is prohibited since 2006 due to unpredictable eruptions that have caused serious scalding injuries. However, the site remains open for viewing from designated areas on the canyon rim, and is a fascinating window into the Mammoth Lakes volcanic field. Swimming is not permitted.
locations.location: Mammoth Lakes area, Mono County (soaking prohibited)

locations.name: Keough's Hot Ditch
locations.description: Keough's Hot Ditch is a free, informal soaking spot just south of Bishop in the Owens Valley, where hot spring water flows through a concrete irrigation ditch before cooling in a series of informal pools. Temperatures near the source reach 110°F and cool progressively downstream. The site sits on BLM land with views of the Sierra Nevada and the White Mountains. Primitive camping is available nearby. A local favorite for its no-frills accessibility.
locations.location: BLM land south of Bishop, Owens Valley, Inyo County

locations.name: Gaviota Hot Springs
locations.description: Gaviota Hot Springs are a pair of small, lithium-rich mineral pools tucked into a canyon within Gaviota State Park along the Santa Barbara coast. A 1-mile round-trip hike leads up a shaded canyon to the springs, which emerge at around 104°F. The pools are small (2–3 people each) and can get crowded on weekends. Park entry fee applies. A wonderful combination of coast and mountain: the Pacific Ocean is visible from higher up the trail.
locations.location: Gaviota State Park, Santa Barbara County

locations.name: Sespe Hot Springs
locations.description: Sespe Hot Springs are among the most remote hot springs in California, requiring a 16–20 mile round-trip hike through the Sespe Wilderness in Los Padres National Forest. The reward is a series of pools ranging from 95–110°F in a dramatic sandstone canyon, with California condors frequently overhead (the nearby Sespe Condor Sanctuary is critical habitat). Plan for an overnight backpack. Permits may be required. This is a true wilderness experience.
locations.location: Sespe Wilderness, Los Padres National Forest, Ventura County

locations.name: Remington Hot Springs
locations.description: Remington Hot Springs sit on the banks of the Kern River in a gorgeous river canyon setting, with two main pools carved into the rocks right at river's edge. Hot spring water seeps through the rocks at around 104°F and mixes with cool river water for adjustable soaking. BLM-managed, free, and open year-round. The site is accessed via a short but steep trail from the road. The Kern River's emerald water and surrounding canyon walls make this spot exceptionally scenic.
locations.location: Kern River Canyon, BLM land, Kern County

locations.name: Miracle Hot Springs
locations.description: Miracle Hot Springs are located just downstream from Remington Hot Springs along the Kern River, offering a similar riverside soaking experience in a slightly more developed setting. Multiple pools of varying temperatures (95–105°F) line the rocky banks of the Kern River. The site was historically a resort and retains some concrete infrastructure. BLM-managed and free to access, it's a popular destination for paddlers who stop here during Kern River kayaking trips.
locations.location: Kern River Canyon, BLM land, Kern County

locations.name: Mono Hot Springs
locations.description: Mono Hot Springs is a small resort community in the Sierra Nevada of Fresno County, perched at 6,500 feet elevation along the South Fork San Joaquin River. The natural hot springs feed a historic resort with private tubs, a bathhouse, and a small general store. Wild soakers will find free pools in the river below the resort. The area sits near Florence Lake and Vermilion Valley Resort at the gateway to the John Muir Wilderness. The drive in via Kaiser Pass Road is scenic but winding.

locations.name: Grover Hot Springs
locations.description: Grover Hot Springs State Park near Markleeville is a classic California hot springs experience: a developed pool maintained at 102–104°F, set in a high mountain meadow surrounded by the Sierra Nevada at 5,900 feet. The park also has a cold pool, campgrounds, and trails into the Toiyabe National Forest. The mineral water is naturally tinged green. Open year-round (conditions permitting), it's a beloved destination for families and winter soakers looking for a structured hot springs outing.
locations.location: Grover Hot Springs State Park, Markleeville, Alpine County

locations.name: Vichy Springs Resort
locations.description: Vichy Springs Resort in Ukiah is California's only naturally carbonated warm mineral springs, operating continuously since 1854. The naturally effervescent 90°F water—sometimes called 'champagne baths'—was visited by Mark Twain, Jack London, Ulysses Grant, and Theodore Roosevelt. The resort offers day use and overnight accommodations. The carbon dioxide-saturated mineral water is said to have therapeutic properties. It is a California Historical Landmark and one of the state's most historically significant spas.
locations.location: Vichy Springs Resort, Ukiah, Mendocino County

locations.name: Old Faithful Geyser of California
locations.description: The Old Faithful Geyser of California in Calistoga is one of only three 'Old Faithful' geysers in the world that erupts on a somewhat regular schedule, shooting a column of 350°F water 60–100 feet into the air roughly every 30 minutes. Located in the Napa Valley geothermal zone, Calistoga itself is renowned for its mud baths and hot springs resorts fed by the same volcanic system. The geyser is a privately operated attraction with an admission fee.

locations.name: Benton Hot Springs
locations.description: Benton Hot Springs is a small historic mining town turned hot springs resort in the remote Mono County high desert, sitting at 5,600 feet between the Sierra Nevada and the White Mountains. The resort offers private outdoor tubs filled with geothermal water ranging from 100–110°F, alongside rustic accommodations including a historic inn and camping. The remote setting, dark skies, and panoramic desert-mountain views make it exceptional for stargazing. Reservations required.

locations.name: Tecopa Hot Springs
locations.description: Tecopa Hot Springs in the Mojave Desert near Death Valley is a small desert community built around natural lithium-rich mineral springs that reach 108°F. The Inyo County parks department operates free outdoor soaking pools segregated by sex, open 24 hours. Numerous private campgrounds and motels cater to the snowbird crowd that descends each winter. The springs have long been rumored to ease arthritis and other ailments. A distinctly quirky and affordable desert soaking destination.

locations.name: Esalen Institute Hot Springs
locations.description: The Esalen Institute hot springs are cliffside baths perched dramatically above the Pacific Ocean at Big Sur, fed by natural hot springs at 104–108°F. Esalen is a world-renowned center for humanistic alternative education, but its famous baths are accessible to the general public only from 1–3 AM for a modest fee — reservations required and book weeks in advance. Clothing is optional. The experience — soaking in mineral water with waves crashing below and stars above — is one of California's most singular.
locations.location: Esalen Institute, Big Sur, Monterey County (public access 1–3 AM only, reservations required)

locations.name: Wister Mud Pot
locations.description: Wister Mud Pot is a natural hot spring catalogued in the USGS Water Supply Paper 338, a comprehensive survey of California geothermal springs. Water temperature reaches 100°F. Located in the Salton Sea area, Imperial County area near the NILAND 7.5 quadrangle. Verify current access, land status, and conditions before visiting — some sites are on private land or require permits.

locations.description: Spring is a natural hot spring catalogued in the USGS Water Supply Paper 338, a comprehensive survey of California geothermal springs. Water temperature reaches 99°F. Located in the Lassen County area near the ALMANOR 15 quadrangle. Verify current access, land status, and conditions before visiting — some sites are on private land or require permits.

locations.name: Above Diamond
locations.description: Above Diamond is a Giant sequoia standing 242.40+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 34,706 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Atwell Mill Grove. Above Diamond, also known as AD, is the world's 26th largest tree, the 24th largest giant redwood, the 13th largest tree in Sequoia National Park, and the 2nd largest tree in Atwell Mill Grove. Naturalists Wendell D. Flint, Jerry Latham, and Michael M. Law named the tree Above Diamond because it grows uphill from (above) Diamond.

locations.name: Above Hazelwood
locations.description: Above Hazelwood is a Giant sequoia standing 265.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 30,017 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Above Hazelwood (the tree is 70 ft (21 m) uphill/southwest from Hazelwood) is the 29th largest tree in Sequoia National Park and the 22nd largest tree in Giant Forest.

locations.name: Adam
locations.description: Adam is a Giant sequoia standing 247.40+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 35,017 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove. Adam is the world's 21st largest tree, the 19th largest giant redwood, the 5th largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument, and the 2nd largest tree in Mountain Home Grove. Rancher Jesse Hoskins named the tree after Adam, the first man on Earth and father of humankind, from the Book of Genesis in the Old Testament.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove

locations.name: Adams
locations.description: Adams is a Giant sequoia standing 250.60+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 38,956 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Adams, also known as Near Cattle Cabin (Adams is 0.14 mi (0.23 km) southwest of Cattle Cabin), is the world's 14th largest tree, the 12th largest giant redwood, the 8th largest tree in Sequoia National Park, and the 6th largest tree in Giant Forest. Naturalists Wendell D. Flint and Michael M. Law named the tree to honor John Adams, the 2nd President of the United States.

locations.name: Adventure
locations.description: Adventure is a Coast redwood standing 332.35+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 32,140 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Otis Grove. Adventure, also known as The Adventure Tree, is the 8th largest coast redwood, the 2nd largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park, and the largest tree in James and Fredda Otis Grove. Naturalist Michael W. Taylor named the tree Adventure because he considered reaching and measuring the tree, which grows on a steep hillside beside a 10 ft (3 m) tall creek bank, an adventure.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Otis Grove

locations.name: Aether's Arrow
locations.description: Aether's Arrow is a Coast redwood standing 365.29+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Calf Creek Flat. Aether's Arrow is the world's 22nd tallest tree, the 13th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods SP, and the 2nd tallest tree on Calf Creek Flat. Aether's Arrow and Stratosphere Giant are just nine trees apart.
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Calf Creek Flat

locations.name: Alder Creek Giant
locations.description: Alder Creek Giant is a Giant sequoia standing 268.50+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 35,694 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Alder Creek Grove. Alder Creek Giant is the world's 19th largest tree, the 17th largest giant redwood, the 4th largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument, and the 2nd largest tree in Alder Creek Grove.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Alder Creek Grove

locations.name: Alice Rhodes
locations.description: Alice Rhodes is a Coast redwood standing 366.60+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Harper Flat. Alice Rhodes is the world's 15th tallest tree, the 9th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park, and the 3rd tallest tree on Harper Flat. Naturalists Ronald W. Hildebrant and Michael W. Taylor named the tree to honor Hildebrant's grandmother.

locations.name: Allen Russell
locations.description: Allen Russell is a Giant sequoia standing 254.10+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 31,650 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove. Allen Russell, also known as Balch Park Tree and Giant of Balch Park, is the 11th largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument and the 6th largest tree in Mountain Home Grove. On June 16, 1990, Tulare County supervisors dedicated the tree to honor Balch Park ranger Allen I. Russell upon his retirement. On June 23, 1991, the Tulare County Historical Society installed a stone marker beside the tree.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove

locations.name: Apex
locations.description: Apex is a Coast redwood standing 368.20+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Harper Flat. Apex, also known as Chevron and Harper Tower, is the world's 12th tallest tree, the 6th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park , and the tallest tree on Harper Flat.

locations.name: ARCo Giant
locations.description: ARCo Giant is a Coast redwood standing 262.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 30,699 ft³. Located in Redwood NP. ARCo Giant, named after Arcata Redwood Company (ARCo) which owned the tree before Redwood National Park was established on October 2, 1968, is the 12th largest coast redwood and the 2nd largest tree in Redwood National Park (excludes Jedediah Smith Redwoods and Prairie Creek Redwoods State Parks). Although ARCo Giant grows near Prairie Creek, the tree is considered part of Redwood National Park because it grows just outside the southern border of Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park.

locations.name: Arm
locations.description: Arm is a Giant sequoia standing 228.00+ ft tall. Located in Sequoia NP, Atwell Mill Grove. Arm has the world's widest-known branch: diameter 9.96 ft (3.04 m). Photographer H. N. Marion discovered the tree in 1936 near the now-defunct Milk Ranch Trail between Oriole Lake and Atwell Mill. Naturalist Wendell D. Flint, who first visted the tree in 1975, named it Arm because he believed the large branch resembles a giant arm.

locations.name: Artemis
locations.description: Artemis is a Coast redwood standing 300.85+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 23,491 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Irvine Grove. Artemis is the 15th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park and the 2nd largest tree in James Irvine Grove. Naturalist Christine A. Aralia named the tree after the Greek Olympian Artemis, goddess of hunting and wilderness, daughter of Zeus. Artemis Creek flows through a small cave within the base of the trunk.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Irvine Grove

locations.name: Atlas
locations.description: Atlas is a Coast redwood standing 299.21+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 25,557 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove. Atlas is the 26th largest coast redwood, the 14th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park, and the 2nd largest tree in Atlas Grove. Atlas is also the 2nd fastest-growing redwood yet measured; it gained an average of 1,698 lb (770 kg) aboveground biomass each year during a recent decade.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove

locations.name: Auto Log
locations.description: Auto Log is a Giant sequoia standing 245.00 ft tall. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. After this previously-unnamed giant redwood fell beside Crescent Meadow Road in February 1917, Sequoia National Park superintendent Walter Fry authorized construction of a trestle, replaced with a driveway a year later, which enabled horse-drawn carriages and automobiles to drive atop the fallen trunk. Auto Log has been a popular tourist attraction ever since. Although Auto Log can no longer support the weight of vehicles, park visitors may still walk the length of the rotten trunk.

locations.name: Ballantine
locations.description: Ballantine is a Coast redwood standing 318.90+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 19,450 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove. Ballantine is the 24th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park and the 8th largest tree in Atlas Grove. Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett named the tree Ballantine to honor his former equipment technician Douglas Ballantine.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove

locations.name: Bannister
locations.description: Bannister is a Giant sequoia standing 195.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 26,100 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Freeman Creek Grove. Bannister, also known as Bannister's Tree, Freeman Snag, and Sequoia Yah, is the 3rd largest tree in Freeman Creek Grove. The tree was named to honor grain merchant and entrepreneur Alfred W. Bannister of Bakersfield. Originally much larger, Bannister lost considerable volume after fire burned the treetop soon after its discovery in 1926. The trunk lost significant height and volume after it burned again in the 2020 Castle Fire, but the tree survived.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Freeman Creek Grove

locations.name: Bell
locations.description: Bell is a Coast redwood standing 314.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 22,079 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove. Bell is the 17th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park and the 4th largest tree in Atlas Grove. Naturalist Michael W. Taylor named the tree Bell-Bottom because its flared base reminded him of bell-bottom trousers. He later shortened the name to Bell at the request of Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett who felt the name Bell-Bottom was unsophisticated.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove

locations.name: Bent Tree
locations.description: Bent Tree is a Giant sequoia standing 235.00+ ft tall. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Wheel Meadow Grove. Bent Tree formed after a lightning strike ignited a fire within the trunk. The upper portion of the trunk collapsed after it burned and then healed in a bent shape. The 2020 Castle Fire destroyed Bent Tree.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Wheel Meadow Grove

locations.name: Big Tree
locations.description: Big Tree is a Coast redwood standing 286.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 28,619 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Irvine Grove. Big Tree, also known as Elk Tree and Elk Grove Tree, is the 16th largest coast redwood, the 7th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park, and the largest tree in James Irvine Grove. Big Tree should not be confused with False Elk (also known as The Parthenon) which grows nearby in Ferguson Grove.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Irvine Grove

locations.name: Black Mountain Beauty
locations.description: Black Mountain Beauty is a Giant sequoia standing 160.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 25,180 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Black Mountain Grove. Before the 2017 Pier Fire, Black Mountain Beauty, also known as Black Mountain Shaft, was the 19th largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument and the largest tree in Black Mountain Grove. The trunk lost 103 ft (31 m) in height and 7,044 ft3 (199 m3) in volume after it burned, and Black Mountain Beauty no longer ranks among the 30 largest giant redwoods in Giant Sequoia National Monument.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Black Mountain Grove

locations.name: Booker T. Washington
locations.description: Located in Sequoia NP. In summer 1903, local civic leaders proposed naming this tree to honor then-Captain (later Colonel then Brigadier General) Charles Young, the first acting superintendent of Sequoia National Park. However, Young insisted they name the tree after influential author and educator Booker T. Washington, whom he had met earlier that year, instead. Sometime after Young and his men formally dedicated this giant redwood, the name tag they had attached to the trunk fell off, and the tree faded into obscurity.

locations.name: Boole
locations.description: Boole is a Giant sequoia standing 268.80+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 44,080 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Converse Basin Grove. Boole, also known as Frank Boole Tree, is the world's 7th largest tree, the 6th largest giant redwood, the 2nd largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument, the largest tree in Converse Basin Grove, and the redwood with the largest footprint (938 ft2 (87.1 m2)).
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Converse Basin Grove

locations.name: Boy Scout
locations.description: Boy Scout is a Coast redwood standing 238.00+ ft tall. Located in Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Metcalf Grove. Boy Scout Tree, named to honor the scouts who first blazed a trail to the tree, is a large, double-stem coast redwood: two fused trees with a combined width over 23 ft (7 m).
locations.location: Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Metcalf Grove

locations.name: Broken Top
locations.description: Broken Top is a Coast redwood standing 265.42+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 11,558 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove. Broken Top is the 13th largest tree in Atlas Grove.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove

locations.name: Brown Creek Giant
locations.description: Brown Creek Giant is a Coast redwood standing 255.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 27,581 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Russ Grove. Brown Creek Giant, named after the nearby creek (although it stands closer to Prairie Creek), is the 20th largest coast redwood, the 9th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park, and the largest tree in Joseph and Zipporah Russ Humboldt County Pioneers Grove.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Russ Grove

locations.name: Brutus
locations.description: Brutus is a Coast redwood standing 361.00+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Harper Flat. Brutus, also known as Big Log, is the 28th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park and the 8th tallest tree on Harper Flat.

locations.name: Buena Vista
locations.description: Buena Vista is a Coast redwood standing 290.03+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 11,375 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove. Buena Vista is the 14th largest tree in Atlas Grove. Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett named the tree Buena Vista, spanish for good view, because it provides a good view of Iluvatar. As documented in the National Geographic Explorer program Climbing Redwood Giants, photographers hoisted cameras into the canopy of Buena Vista to create the composite photograph of Iluvatar published in the October 2009 issue of National Geographic magazine.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove

locations.name: Buffalo Chip
locations.description: Located in Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP. Buffalo Chip is a multi-stem coast redwood with a disctinctive basal burl which resembles buffalo manure.

locations.name: Bull Buck
locations.description: Bull Buck is a Giant sequoia standing 246.10+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 27,383 ft³. Located in Sierra NF, Nelder Grove. Bull Buck is the 3rd largest tree in Sierra National Forest and the 3rd largest tree in Nelder Grove. The naming of Bull Buck remains controversial. Some suggest the tree was named to honor a felling and bucking foreman (also known as the bull buck or woods boss) who reportedly instructed his crew to spare the tree during logging operations in the 1880s. Others claim the tree was so named because its enormous size makes it appear to be the boss of the woods. Bull Buck is 1.2 ft (0.4 m) shorter and 1,707 ft3 (48 m3) smaller than a nearby sign indicates: 247.31 ft (75.38 m) and 29,090 ft3 (824 m

locations.name: Bull Creek Giant
locations.description: Bull Creek Giant is a Coast redwood standing 336.29+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 31,146 ft³. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Middle Bull Creek Flat. Bull Creek Giant, also known as Gaia, is the 9th largest coast redwood and the largest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park.
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Middle Bull Creek Flat

locations.name: Burgeon
locations.description: Burgeon is a Giant sequoia standing 285.27+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 25,533 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Burgeon is the fastest-growing giant redwood yet measured. The tree gained an average of 1,349 lb/y (612 kg/y) aboveground biomass each year during a recent decade. The crown contains nearly one-and-a-half billion leaves and ninety-three thousand cones.

locations.name: Bushy Toe
locations.description: Bushy Toe is a Coast redwood standing 351.31+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Harper Flat. Naturalist Michael W. Taylor named the tree Bushy Toe because it has a large, toe-like protuberance at its base.

locations.name: California
locations.description: California is a Giant sequoia standing 249.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 34,723 ft³. Located in Kings Canyon NP, General Grant Grove. California is the world's 25th largest tree, the 23rd largest giant redwood, the 5th largest tree in Kings Canyon National Park, and the 4th largest tree in General Grant Grove.
locations.location: Kings Canyon NP, General Grant Grove

locations.name: California
locations.description: California is a Giant sequoia standing 232.00+ ft tall. Located in Yosemite NP, Mariposa Grove. California is the last surviving giant redwood tunnel tree. In 1895, the Yosemite Stage and Turnpike Company hired W. H. Coop and John Cummings to carve a tunnel through the base of the tree to allow tourists passage through an alternate tunnel tree in the lower Mariposa Grove whenever snowpack blocked the route to Wawona Tunnel Tree in the upper grove. California remained a drive-through tree from 1895 until 1932 when the National Park Service rerouted Mariposa Grove Road away from the base of Grizzly Giant and converted to a footpath the segment of abandoned road which had passed through Cal

locations.name: Candelabra
locations.description: Candelabra is a Giant sequoia standing 228.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 26,341 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Packsaddle Grove. Before it perished in the 2021 Windy Fire, Candelabra, also known as Turnip, was the 2nd largest tree in Packsaddle Grove. Naturalists Wendell D. Flint and Michael Law named the tree Turnip when they discovered it in 1971 because the trunk reminded them of a huge turnip. Naturalist William Croft renamed the tree Candelabra in 1978 because he felt it more resembled a candelabra.

locations.name: Canoe Creek
locations.description: Canoe Creek is a Coast redwood standing 361.29+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP. Canoe Creek is the 27th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park and the tallest tree in Garden Club of America Grove.

locations.name: Castro
locations.description: Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Freeman Creek Grove. Castro, named for Ralph Castro who carved his name inside the trunk in 1939, is a large, fire-hollowed giant redwood. Naturalist Wendell D. Flint described Castro as a great Telescope Tree and the most impressive tree of its kind he had seen. After it burned in the 2020 Castle Fire, Castro is now just a redwood snag, and the historical trunk carvings are no longer visible.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Freeman Creek Grove

locations.name: Chandelier
locations.description: Chandelier is a Coast redwood standing 282.00+ ft tall. Chandelier, also known as Drive-Thru Tree and Major Hendricks Tree, is the second coast redwood tunnel tree. Located at 67402 Drive-Thru Tree Road in Leggett, California 1.0 mi (1.6 km) south of the northern terminus of California State Route 1 at U.S. Highway 101, Chandelier was so named because it resembles an upside down chandelier with enormous branches balanced on each side of the trunk. These limbs, which measure from 4.0 to 7.0 ft (1.2 to 2.1 m) in diameter, emerge from the main trunk approximately 100 ft (30.5 m) above ground.

locations.name: Chesty Puller
locations.description: Chesty Puller is a Coast redwood standing 318.00+ ft tall. Located in Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Grove of Titans. Chesty Puller, also known as Fused Titan, is a large, multi-stem coast redwood near the southern edge of the Grove of Titans. The tree was renamed to honor United States Marine Corps Lieutenant General Lewis Burwell Chesty Puller, the most-decorated Marine in history.
locations.location: Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Grove of Titans

locations.name: Chief Sequoyah
locations.description: Chief Sequoyah is a Giant sequoia standing 228.20+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 33,608 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Chief Sequoyah is the 29th largest giant redwood, the 14th largest tree in Sequoia National Park, and the 10th largest tree in Giant Forest. In 1928, Sequoia National Park superintendent Colonel John R. White named the tree to honor Chief Sequoyah of the Cherokee Nation who, in the early 1800s, created the Cherokee alphabet. In July 1931, the Appropriation Committee of the United States Department of the Interior formally dedicated the tree as Chief Sequoyah during a ceremony beside the tree.

locations.name: Chimney
locations.description: Located in Sequoia NP. Chimney is a hollow, giant redwood snag. Once considered a major attraction in Sequoia National Park, Chimney was a living telescope tree, whose trunk was hollow from top to bottom, until a campfire destroyed it in 1919.

locations.name: Church Tree
locations.description: Trunk volume: 23,000 ft³. Located in Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, National Tribute Grove. Church Tree contains a fire-hollowed chamber which measures 20 ft (6 m) across and 43 ft (13 m) tall. Local residents have camped and even held wedding ceremonies inside this tree.
locations.location: Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, National Tribute Grove

locations.name: Clara Barton
locations.description: Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Members of the Kaweah Cooperative Commonwealth, a socialist colony established along the North Fork of Kaweah River before Sequoia National Park was established on September 25, 1890, named the tree to honor philanthropist Clarissa (Clara) H. Barton, founder and first president of the American Red Cross.

locations.name: Cleveland
locations.description: Cleveland is a Giant sequoia standing 250.50+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 31,336 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Cleveland is the 22nd largest tree in Sequoia National Park, the 16th largest tree in Giant Forest, and the 4th oldest living redwood. In 1902, Sequoia National Park concessionaire John Broder named the tree to honor Grover Cleveland, the 22nd President of the United States.

locations.name: Columbia
locations.description: Columbia is a Giant sequoia standing 280.50+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 26,238 ft³. Located in Yosemite NP, Mariposa Grove. Columbia, named to honor Columbia University, is the 6th largest tree in Yosemite National Park and the 2nd tallest and 6th largest tree in Mariposa Grove.

locations.name: Column
locations.description: Column is a Giant sequoia standing 243.80+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 37,295 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Column, also known as Near Pershing (Column is 350 ft (107 m) southwest of General Pershing), is the world's 15th largest tree, the 13th largest giant redwood, the 9th largest tree in Sequoia National Park, and the 7th largest tree in Giant Forest. Naturalists Wendell D. Flint and Michael M. Law named the tree Column because they believed the trunk resembles a massive, wooden column.

locations.name: Conehead
locations.description: Conehead is a Giant sequoia standing 281.33+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 16,951 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Freeman Creek Grove. Conehead is the most-fecund redwood yet measured. The crown, which contains more than one billion leaves, includes nearly one-hundred-eleven thousand cones: thirty-one thousand and thirty-seven thousand, respectively, more than the considerably-larger giant redwoods President and General Sherman.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Freeman Creek Grove

locations.name: Corkscrew
locations.description: Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Otis Grove. The National Park Service asserts that Corkscrew is an example of a cathedral tree, or fairy ring, similar to Titanic Cathedral. Others suspect that the intertwined trunks formed after several coast redwoods, which grew around a central tree (perhaps of a different species) that later decayed, were buffeted by storms.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Otis Grove

locations.name: Crescent Meadow Road
locations.description: Crescent Meadow Road is a Giant sequoia standing 258.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 27,734 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Crescent Meadow Road Tree, also known as Moro Road Tree, is the 27th largest tree in Giant Forest.

locations.name: Crown Jewel
locations.description: Crown Jewel is a Coast redwood standing 363.10+ ft tall. Located in Redwood NP, Hyperion Valley. Crown Jewel is the 10th tallest tree in Redwood National Park and the 2nd tallest tree in Hyperion Valley. National Geographic photographer Michael K. Nichols photographed Tom McDonald from Crown Jewel. Videographer Steven A. Hall Jr. documented a hike up Tom McDonald Creek to Crown Jewel.

locations.name: Daedalus
locations.description: Daedalus is a Coast redwood standing 363.39+ ft tall. Located in Redwood NP, Helios Grove. Daedalus is the world's 30th tallest tree, the 9th tallest tree in Redwood National Park, and the 3rd tallest tree in Helios Grove. Naturalists Chris K. Atkins and Michael W. Taylor named the tree after Daedalus, father of Icarus and creator of The Labyrinth, from Greek mythology. Elam Creek is also known as Dry Heaves Creek.

locations.name: Dalton
locations.description: Dalton is a Giant sequoia standing 274.50+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 31,065 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Muir Grove. Dalton, also known as Giant of Muir Grove, is the 24th largest tree in Sequoia National Park and the largest tree in Muir Grove.

locations.name: Dead Giant
locations.description: Located in Yosemite NP. Dead Giant, also known as King Solomon's Temple, is the first redwood tunnel tree. Dentist turned prospector James L. Cogswell discovered Tuolomne Grove on May 10, 1858 and named the giant redwood snag King Solomon's Temple. In 1878, David and James J. Lumsden and William McCarthy carved a 10 ft (3 m)-wide, 12 ft (4 m)-tall tunnel through the dead trunk so that wagons and, later, automobiles could drive through it. Dead Giant, as it came to be known sometime later, remained a drive-through tree from 1878 until 1993 when the National Park Service closed the grove to automobile traffic.

locations.name: Dean
locations.description: Dean is a Giant sequoia standing 235.80+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 32,333 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Atwell Mill Grove. Dean is the 19th largest tree in Sequoia National Park and the 3rd largest tree in Atwell Mill Grove. In 1950, naturalist Wendell D. Flint named the tree for an early pioneer who had carved the name Dean on a charred area of the trunk. By 1983, the inscription was no longer discernable.

locations.name: Del Norte Titan
locations.description: Del Norte Titan is a Coast redwood standing 306.76+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 32,535 ft³. Located in Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Grove of Titans. Del Norte Titan is the 7th largest coast redwood, the 5th largest tree in Jedediah Smith Redwoods State Park, the 3rd largest tree in National Tribute Grove, and the 2nd largest tree in the Grove of Titans. Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett and naturalist Michael W. Taylor named the tree after the surrounding Del Norte County.
locations.location: Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Grove of Titans

locations.name: Demeter
locations.description: Demeter is a Coast redwood standing 319.88+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 13,640 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove. Demeter is the 28th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park and the 10th largest tree in Atlas Grove. Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett named the tree after the Greek Olympian Demeter, goddess of agriculture, daughter of Kronos and Rhea and sister of Poseidon and Zeus.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove

locations.name: Diamond
locations.description: Diamond is a Giant sequoia standing 286.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 35,292 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Atwell Mill Grove. Before the 2021 KNP Complex Fire, Diamond was the world's 20th largest tree, the 18th largest giant redwood, the 11th largest tree in Sequoia National Park, and the largest tree in Atwell Mill Grove. The trunk lost significant volume after it burned, and, pending updated volume estimates, Diamond likely no longer ranks among the 30 largest giant redwoods.

locations.name: Dyerville Giant
locations.description: Dyerville Giant is a Coast redwood standing 372.00++ ft tall. Trunk volume: 27,088 ft³. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Founders Grove. University of California at Berkeley forestry professor Paul J. Zinke and graduate student Alan G. Stangenberger discovered Dyerville Giant in 1966 and named the tree after the nearby, defunct town of Dyerville. Although Zinke and others suspected, as early as 1966, that Howard Libbey was no longer world's tallest, nobody knew that Dyerville Giant had been the new height champion until naturalists Michael W. Taylor and Ron Hildebrant estimated its height from marks made on a nearby tree when it fell on March 25, 1991. The tree was more than 10 ft (3 m) taller than a nearby sign once indicated.

locations.name: Ed by Ned
locations.description: Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Ed by Ned, also known as Uncle Ned Tree, is a pair of intertwined giant redwoods, whose combined base measures 34 ft (10 m) long and 25 ft (8 m) wide, near the southern end of Round Meadow in Giant Forest.

locations.name: El Palo Alto
locations.description: El Palo Alto is a Coast redwood standing 110.00+ ft tall. El Palo Alto (Spanish for the tall tree) is the first named tree in America and the first official, living California landmark (California Historical Landmark No. 2). This coast redwood originally had twin trunks (then known as Palos Colorados, Spanish for red trees), but, in 1886, one trunk vanished. Some claim it fell during a windstorm and flood, but others suspect Southern Pacific Railroad cut it down during construction of the bridge over San Francisquito Creek.

locations.name: El Viejo del Norte
locations.description: El Viejo del Norte is a Coast redwood standing 323.82+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 30,784 ft³. Located in Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Grove of Titans. El Viejo del Norte, Spanish for the old man of the north, is the 11th largest coast redwood, the 6th largest tree in Jedediah Smith Redwoods State Park, the 4th largest tree in National Tribute Grove, and the 3rd largest tree in the Grove of Titans. Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett and naturalist Michael W. Taylor named the tree El Viejo del Norte given its gnarled appearance.
locations.location: Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Grove of Titans

locations.name: Elk Herd
locations.description: Located in Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, National Tribute Grove. Elk Herd is a multi-stem coast redwood with a combined basal diameter of 34 ft (10 m).
locations.location: Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, National Tribute Grove

locations.name: Emerald Giant
locations.description: Emerald Giant is a Coast redwood standing 359.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 17,930 ft³. Located in Redwood NP. Emerald Giant, also known as Emerald Springs Tree, was named after nearby Emerald Creek (also known as Harry Weir Creek). Emerald Giant is the 8th largest and 16th tallest tree in Redwood National Park (excludes Jedediah Smith Redwoods and Prairie Creek Redwoods State Parks) and the tallest and largest tree in Emerald Grove. Emerald Giant is also the 3rd fastest-growing redwood yet measured; it gained an average of 1,627 lb (738 kg) aboveground biomass each year during a recent decade.

locations.name: Epimetheus
locations.description: Epimetheus is a Coast redwood standing 295.60+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 20,329 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove. Epimetheus is the 23rd largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park and the 7th largest tree in Atlas Grove. Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett named the tree after the Greek Titan Epimetheus, god of hindsight, brother of Atlas and Prometheus.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove

locations.name: Eric De Groot
locations.description: Eric De Groot is a Giant sequoia standing 262.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 31,974 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Garfield Grove. Eric De Groot is the 21st largest tree in Sequoia National Park and the 3rd largest tree in Garfield Grove. The tree was named to honor Calvin College student Eric J. De Groot of Visalia who died unexpectedly on August 19, 2013. Friends attached a small Eric De Groot Tree sign to the trunk after his death, but the sign has since disappeared.

locations.name: Euclid
locations.description: Euclid is a Giant sequoia standing 272.70+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 36,122 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove. Euclid is the the world's 16th largest tree, the 14th largest giant redwood, the 3rd largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument, and the largest tree in Mountain Home Grove. Naturalists Wendell D. Flint and Robert Walker named the tree Euclid to honor the Greek mathematician Euclid, the father of geometry.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove

locations.name: Fallen Monarch
locations.description: Fallen Monarch is a Giant sequoia standing 125.00+ ft tall. Located in Kings Canyon NP, General Grant Grove. Fallen Monarch, also known as Dead Monarch, is a giant redwood log which was hollowed out by fire before it fell more than 300 years ago. Cattlemen Israel and Thomas Gamlin, who lived within General Grant Grove from 1868 to 1878, used Fallen Monarch as a temporary shelter while they built Gamlin Pioneer Cabin nearby. Later, Fallen Monarch became a local hotel and saloon and, from 1890 to 1914, served as a stable for the United States Cavalry which patrolled General Grant National Park (now part of Kings Canyon National Park) after it was established on October 1, 1890.
locations.location: Kings Canyon NP, General Grant Grove

locations.name: False Elk
locations.description: False Elk is a Coast redwood standing 311.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 27,944 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP. False Elk, also known as The Parthenon, is the 18th largest coast redwood, the 8th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park, and the largest tree in Vira Georgeson and William C. Ferguson Grove. False Elk should not be confused with Big Tree (also known as Elk Tree and Elk Grove Tree) which grows nearby in James Irvine Grove.

locations.name: False Jupiter
locations.description: Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Volkmann Grove. Although it has a wider base than that of Jupiter, which grows in National Tribute Grove in Jedediah Smith Redwoods State Park, False Jupiter is not the widest-known redwood. Its base is a fusion of two trees oriented north to south which is most-apparent when viewed from east or west.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Volkmann Grove

locations.name: Fesler
locations.description: Fesler is a Coast redwood standing 215.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 21,470 ft³. Located in Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP. Fesler, also known as Fessler and Simpson-Reed Giant, is the 11th largest tree in Jedediah Smith Redwoods State Park and the largest tree in Ruth Adams Skinner and Leona Ann Stelter Redwood Grove. Athough the tree was named for nearby David R. and Sherrill E. Fesler Grove and Simpson-Reed Grove, it actually stands within Ruth Adams Skinner and Leona Ann Stelter Redwood Grove.

locations.name: Floyd Otter
locations.description: Floyd Otter is a Giant sequoia standing 273.10+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 39,562 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Garfield Grove. Before the 2020 Castle Fire, Floyd Otter, named to honor former Mountain Home Demonstration State Forest manager Floyd L. Otter, was the world's 13th largest tree, the 11th largest giant redwood, the 7th largest tree in Sequoia National Park, and the 2nd largest tree in Garfield Grove. The trunk lost significant volume after it burned, and, pending updated volume estimates, Floyd Otter likely no longer ranks among the 30 largest giant redwoods.

locations.name: Foothill
locations.description: Foothill is a Coast redwood standing 301.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 26,500 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Russ Grove. Foothill, named after the nearby Foothill Trail, is the 21st largest coast redwood, the 10th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park, and the 2nd largest tree in Joseph and Zipporah Russ Humboldt County Pioneers Grove.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Russ Grove

locations.name: Founders
locations.description: Founders is a Coast redwood standing 325.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 10,417 ft³. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Founders Grove. Founders, also known as Fifth Tallest Tree (1963), was named to honor paleontologist John Campbell Merriam, attorney Madison Grant, and geologist/paleontologist Henry Fairfield Osborn who founded Save the Redwoods League in 1918. Founders held the title of world's tallest tree from 1931 until 1957 when Humboldt State University forestry professor Gerald L. Partain discovered Rockefeller in Humboldt Redwoods State Park.

locations.name: Founders Group
locations.description: Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Founders Group, also known as Entente Allies, is a cluster of giant redwoods located between Adams and Room in Giant Forest. On July 8, 1931, Sequoia National Park superintendent Colonel John R. White dedicated Founders Group to honor park founders Henry E. Barbour, Tipton Lindsey, John F. Miller, George W. Stewart (known as The Father of Sequoia National Park, John Tuohy, William Vandever, Frederick H. Wales, Frank J. Walker, William B. Wallace, and Daniel K. Zumwalt whose determination to preserve giant redwoods in Fresno and Tulare counties led to the establishment of Sequoia National Park.

locations.name: Four Guardsmen
locations.description: Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. The Four Guardsmen, also known as the Four Guardians and the Gateway Group, is a colonnade of four giant redwoods which spans California State Route 198 (Generals Highway) near the southern edge of Giant Forest. As the first group of giant redwoods visible to motorists driving north on Generals Highway from the Sequoia National Park entrance station, the Four Guardsmen form a striking, natural gateway to Giant Forest. George Welsh named the trees in 1919 while surveying the route for Generals Highway.

locations.name: Franklin
locations.description: Franklin is a Giant sequoia standing 223.80+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 43,440 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Franklin, also known as Near Washington (Franklin is 630 ft (192 m) southeast of Washington), is the world's 8th largest tree, the 7th largest giant redwood, the 4th largest tree in Sequoia National Park, and the 4th largest tree in Giant Forest. Naturalist Wendell D. Flint named the tree to honor Benjamin Franklin, a Founding Father of the United States.

locations.name: Galen Clark
locations.description: Galen Clark is a Giant sequoia standing 239.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 21,764 ft³. Located in Yosemite NP, Mariposa Grove. Galen Clark Tree, named to honor Galen Clark, the first Guardian of the Yosemite Grant (which became Yosemite National Park on October 1, 1890), is the 12th largest tree in Yosemite National Park and the 11th largest tree in Mariposa Grove. Clark and his associate, Milton Mann, first entered the upper grove near Galen Clark Tree (Clark noted later that they had visited Vermont Tree first) when they discovered Mariposa Grove in May 1857.

locations.name: Gamato
locations.description: Trunk volume: 20,775 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Goethe Grove. Gamato is also known as Newton B. Drury Scenic Parkway Tree.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Goethe Grove

locations.name: Gemini
locations.description: Gemini is a Coast redwood standing 345.00+ ft tall. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Otis Grove. Gemini is the 11th tallest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park and the tallest tree in James and Fredda Otis Grove. Gemini appears (from 3:48 to 3:55 and 10:28 to 10:46) in the National Geographic Explorer program Climbing Redwood Giants.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Otis Grove

locations.name: General Grant
locations.description: General Grant is a Giant sequoia standing 268.10+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 47,930 ft³. Located in Kings Canyon NP, General Grant Grove. General Grant, named to honor Ulysses S. Grant, Commanding General of the United States Army and 18th President of the United States, is the world's 2nd largest tree, the widest and 2nd largest giant redwood, and the largest tree in Kings Canyon National Park.
locations.location: Kings Canyon NP, General Grant Grove

locations.name: General Harbord
locations.description: General Harbord is a Giant sequoia standing 267.60+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 25,798 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. In 1926, Sequoia National Park superintendent Colonel John R. White dedicated General Harbord tree to honor General James G. Harbord who served as Chief of Staff for General John J. Pershing, Commander of the American Expeditionary Force, during World War 1.

locations.name: General Lee
locations.description: General Lee is a Giant sequoia standing 254.50+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 26,040 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. In 1901, Sequoia National Park concessionaire John Broder named the tree to honor Confederate Army General Robert E. Lee. On July 1, 1937, the United Daughters of the Confederacy formally dedicated the tree as General Lee during a ceremony beside the tree.

locations.name: General Pershing
locations.description: General Pershing is a Giant sequoia standing 246.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 35,855 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. General Pershing is the world's 18th largest tree, the 16th largest giant redwood, the 10th largest tree in Sequoia National Park, and the 8th largest tree in Giant Forest. In 1926, Sequoia National Park superintendent Colonel John R. White dedicated the tree to honor General John J. Pershing, Commander of the American Expeditionary Force during World War 1.

locations.name: General Sheridan
locations.description: General Sheridan is a Giant sequoia standing 254.50+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 20,228 ft³. Located in Yosemite NP, Mariposa Grove. General Sheridan, named to honor United States Army Civil War General Philip H. Sheridan and also known as William H. Seward Tree, is the 15th largest tree in Yosemite National Park and the 14th largest tree in Mariposa Grove. In May 1864, naturalist and future Yosemite Grant Guardian Galen Clark built a cabin in the meadow near General Sheridan. In 1931, the National Park Service replaced Clark's cabin with the current Mariposa Grove Cabin which served as a museum and gift shop until July 2015.

locations.name: General Sherman
locations.description: General Sherman is a Giant sequoia standing 274.25+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 49,264 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. General Sherman, named to honor United States Union Army General William T. Sherman, is the largest of three trillion trees on Earth, the current national champion giant redwood (1325 points), and the 2nd oldest living redwood. It has more than two billion leaves (most of any tree yet measured) and seventy-four thousand cones and is the only known tree which has a trunk 20 ft (6 m) wide 35 ft (11 m) above ground.

locations.name: General Young
locations.description: General Young is a Giant sequoia standing 250.00+ ft tall. Located in Sequoia NP. Dedicated on July 28, 2024, General Young Tree was named to honor Brigadier General Charles Young who, in 1903, served as the first acting superintendent of Sequoia National Park. Then-Captain Young and fellow Buffalo soldiers from Troops I and M of the United States Army's 9th Cavalry Regiment managed, maintained, and patrolled the park during the summer of 1903. Young and his men not only protected the park from loggers, poachers, and grazing livestock but also built hiking trails and completed the first wagon roads to Giant Forest and to the base of Moro Rock.

locations.name: Genesis
locations.description: Genesis is a Giant sequoia standing 214.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 34,598 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove. Before the 2020 Castle Fire, Genesis, also known as Dogwood Meadow Tree, was the world's 9th largest tree, the 8th largest giant redwood, the 3rd largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument, and the largest tree in Mountain Home Grove. The trunk lost 7,299 ft3 (207 m3) volume and 39 ft (12 m) height after it burned, and Genesis is now the world's 27th largest tree, the 25th largest giant redwood, the 6th largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument, and the 3rd largest tree in Mountain Home Grove.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove

locations.name: George Bush
locations.description: George Bush is a Giant sequoia standing 245.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 16,973 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Freeman Creek Grove. George Bush Tree was named to honor George H. W. Bush, the 41st President of the United States, who, on July 14, 1992 during a ceremony beside the tree, signed Proclamation 6457 to protect several groves of old-growth giant redwoods within Sequoia, Sierra, and Tahoe National Forests (which later became part of Giant Sequoia National Monument) for the benefit and enjoyment of present and future generations.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Freeman Creek Grove

locations.name: Ghost
locations.description: Ghost is a Giant sequoia standing 180.60+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 25,047 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Packsaddle Grove. Before the 2021 Windy Fire, Ghost, also known as General Robert E. Lee, was the 3rd largest tree in Packsaddle Grove. The trunk lost significant height and volume after it burned, and, pending updated volume estimates, Ghost likely no longer ranks among the largest trees in Packsaddle Grove.

locations.name: Giant Tree
locations.description: Giant Tree is a Coast redwood standing 354.30+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 27,800 ft³. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Upper Bull Creek Flat. Giant Tree, also known as Big Tree (not to be confused with Big Tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park) and Bull Creek Tree, is the 19th largest coast redwood, the 2nd largest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park, and the largest tree on Upper Bull Creek Flat.
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Upper Bull Creek Flat

locations.name: Godwood Creek Giant
locations.description: Godwood Creek Giant is a Coast redwood standing 353.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 25,636 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Walker Grove. Godwood Creek Giant is the 24th largest coast redwood, the 7th tallest and 12th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park, and the tallest and 2nd largest tree in Clinton L. Walker Grove. Once 361 ft (110 m) tall, Godwood Creek Giant lost height when part of its dead top broke off in the late 1990's.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Walker Grove

locations.name: Gothic Arch
locations.description: Gothic Arch is a Giant sequoia standing 249.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 30,036 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Gothic Arch, also known as Alta Twin 2, is the 28th largest tree in Sequoia National Park and the 21st largest tree in Giant Forest.

locations.name: Gray Poison
locations.description: Gray Poison is a Coast redwood standing 362.70+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Middle Bull Creek Flat. Gray Poison is the 22nd tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park and the 5th tallest tree on Middle Bull Creek Flat. Naturalists Ronald W. Hildebrant and Michael W. Taylor named the tree Gray Poison because it has gray-colored bark covered with poison oak vines.
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Middle Bull Creek Flat

locations.name: Great Bonsai
locations.description: Great Bonsai is a Giant sequoia standing 184.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 20,631 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove. Naturalist Wendell D. Flint described Great Bonsai as one of the great sequoia sights to be seen anywhere ... a monstrous sequoia with many huge limbs almost extending to the ground. The 2020 Castle Fire destroyed Great Bonsai.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove

locations.name: Great Goshawk
locations.description: Great Goshawk is a Giant sequoia standing 255.20+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 32,783 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Freeman Creek Grove. Before the 2020 Castle Fire, Great Goshawk was the 9th largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument and the largest tree in Freeman Creek Grove. Naturalist William Croft named the tree after an angry northern goshawk (Accipiter gentilis) which swooped down upon him when he first visited the tree. The trunk lost significant height and volume after it burned, and, pending updated volume estimates, Great Goshawk likely no longer ranks among the 30 largest giant redwoods.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Freeman Creek Grove

locations.name: Grizzly Giant
locations.description: Grizzly Giant is a Giant sequoia standing 208.33+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 31,501 ft³. Located in Yosemite NP, Mariposa Grove. Grizzly Giant, formerly known as Grizzled Giant, is the 3rd largest tree in Yosemite National Park, the 3rd largest tree in Mariposa Grove, and the 3rd oldest living redwood. Publisher James M. Hutchings, at the suggestion of naturalist Galen Clark, named the tree Grizzled Giant in 1859. The tree was renamed Grizzly Giant sometime later.

locations.name: Gulch Tower
locations.description: Gulch Tower is a Coast redwood standing 363.42+ ft tall. Located in Redwood NP, Orion Grove. Gulch Tower, also known as T7, is the world's 29th tallest tree, the 8th tallest tree in Redwood National Park, the 2nd tallest tree in Orion Grove, and the 2nd tallest tree discovered by light detection and ranging (LiDAR). Elam Creek is also known as Dry Heaves Creek.

locations.name: Gutless Goliath
locations.description: Gutless Goliath is a Giant sequoia standing 275.10+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 26,564 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Wheel Meadow Grove. Before it perished in the 2020 Castle Fire, Gutless Goliath was the 2nd largest tree in Wheel Meadow Grove.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Wheel Meadow Grove

locations.name: Hail Storm
locations.description: Hail Storm is a Coast redwood standing 321.19+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 44,750 ft³. Located in Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, National Tribute Grove. Hail Storm, also known as The Wall and Girl Scout Tree (because it resembles Boy Scout Tree nearby), is the world's 4th largest tree and the largest coast redwood. Hail Storm has the lowest-density wood of any coast redwood yet measured.
locations.location: Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, National Tribute Grove

locations.name: Hamilton
locations.description: Hamilton is a Giant sequoia standing 238.50+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 32,783 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Hamilton, also known as Near Triple Tree (Hamilton is 510 ft (155 m) west of Triple Tree), is the 16th largest tree in Sequoia National Park and the 12th largest tree in Giant Forest. In 1993, naturalists Wendell D. Flint and Michael M. Law named the tree to honor Alexander Hamilton, a Founding Father of the United States and 1st Secretary of the Treasury.

locations.name: Harriett Weaver
locations.description: Harriett Weaver is a Coast redwood standing 364.90+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Middle Bull Creek Flat. Harriett Weaver is the world's 23rd tallest tree, the 14th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park, and the 3rd tallest tree in Middle Bull Creek Flat. Naturalists Ronald W. Hildebrant and Michael W. Taylor named the tree to honor former California State Parks ranger Harriett E. Weaver.
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Middle Bull Creek Flat

locations.name: Harry Cole
locations.description: Harry Cole is a Coast redwood standing 366.11+ ft tall. Located in Redwood NP, Forty Four Grove. Harry Cole, also known as Second Tallest Tree (1963), is the world's 18th tallest tree, the 7th tallest tree in Redwood National Park, and the tallest tree in Forty Four Grove. Named after Harry W. Cole, an officer of the Little River Redwood Company, Harry Cole held the title of world's tallest tree from July 1988 until May 1991 when naturalist Ron Hildebrant remeasured Nugget in Redwood National Park. Like Howard Libbey, Harry Cole lost height from storm damage, dieback, and decades of silt deposition around its trunk. Harry Cole is now 1.3 ft (0.4 m) shorter than a nearby sign indicates: 36

locations.name: Hart
locations.description: Hart is a Giant sequoia standing 277.90+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 34,407 ft³. Located in Kings Canyon NP, Redwood Mountain Grove. Before the 2021 KNP Complex Fire, Hart was the world's 30th largest tree, the 27th largest giant redwood, the 6th largest tree in Kings Canyon National Park, and the 2nd largest tree in Redwood Mountain Grove. Once considered a contender for world's largest tree, Hart was identified as 4th largest in 1931 when a team of engineers, led by J. W. Jourdan of the San Joaquin Light and Power Corporation, measured the four then-largest-known redwoods (Boole, Hart, General Grant, and General Sherman) and determined that General Sherman was the true size champion. Other giant redwoods have since surpas
locations.location: Kings Canyon NP, Redwood Mountain Grove

locations.name: Hazelwood
locations.description: Hazelwood is a Giant sequoia standing 229.00 ft tall. Trunk volume: 32,610 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Hazelwood, named for the nearby Hazelwood Nature Trail, was once considered the world's 17th largest tree (trunk volume of 36,228 ft3 (1,026 m3)). In June 2002, Hazelwood lost over 3,600 ft3 (102 m3) in volume after a lightning strike. Hazelwood remains the 17th largest tree in Sequoia National Park and the 13th largest tree in Giant Forest.

locations.name: Hazelwood Pull-Off
locations.description: Hazelwood Pull-Off is a Giant sequoia standing 269.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 30,329 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Hazelwood Pull-Off is the 27th largest tree in Sequoia National Park and the 20th largest tree in Giant Forest.

locations.name: Helios
locations.description: Helios is a Coast redwood standing 377.13+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 20,500 ft³. Located in Redwood NP, Helios Grove. Helios is the world's 2nd tallest tree, the 2nd tallest and 6th largest tree in Redwood National Park, and the tallest and largest tree in Helios Grove. Naturalists Chris K. Atkins and Michael W. Taylor named the tree after the Greek Titan Helios, god of the sun and son of Hyperion, because its treetop remained sunlit when first spotted near sunset on July 1, 2006. Helios held the title of world's tallest tree from July 1, 2006 until August 25, 2006 when Atkins and Taylor discovered Hyperion in Redwood National Park. Elam Creek is also known as Dry Heaves Creek.

locations.name: Hercules
locations.description: Hercules is a Giant sequoia standing 169.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 22,042 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove. Rancher Jesse Hoskins named the tree after the Roman god Hercules also known as the Greek god Heracles, son of Zeus. In 1897, Hoskins drilled and carved a 12 ft (4 m) diameter and 9 ft (3 m) tall chamber inside the trunk and added stairs, a wooden door, and a sign. He later used the room as a store to sell redwood souvenirs made from the carving remnants. Vandals removed the door and the sign sometime later and even lit a fire inside, but the tree and the room survived.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove

locations.name: Hill-Davis
locations.description: Hill-Davis is a Coast redwood standing 344.59+ ft tall. Located in Redwood NP, Tall Trees Grove. Hill-Davis, also known as Sixth Tallest Tree (1963), is the 10th tallest tree in Tall Trees Grove. Hill-Davis was named after the Hill-Davis Company, a subsidiary of Weyerhaeuser and parent of Arcata Redwood Company (ARCo), which owned Tall Trees Grove, also known as Arcata Redwood Company (ARCo) Grove of the Giants and Glen of Giants, before Redwood National Park was established on October 2, 1968.

locations.name: Howard Libbey
locations.description: Howard Libbey is a Coast redwood standing 362.80+ ft tall. Located in Redwood NP, Tall Trees Grove. Howard Libbey, also known as Tall Tree, is the 11th tallest tree in Redwood National Park and the 3rd tallest tree in Tall Trees Grove. Named for Howard A. Libbey, founding president of the Arcata Redwood Company (ARCo), Howard Libbey held the title of world's tallest tree from 1963 until July 1988 when naturalist Ron Hildebrant remeasured Harry Cole in Redwood National Park.

locations.name: Howland Hill Giant
locations.description: Howland Hill Giant is a Coast redwood standing 330.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 33,581 ft³. Located in Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP. Howland Hill Giant, also known as Howland Drive Tree and Weyand Tree, is the 4th largest coast redwood, the 4th largest tree in Jedediah Smith Redwoods State Park, and the largest tree in Goble Grove. California State Parks ranger Daniel M. Weyand discovered the tree beside Howland Hill Road in 1970. Howland Hill Giant is the only known tree which has a trunk 6 ft (2 m) wide 300 ft (91 m) above ground.

locations.name: Hyperion
locations.description: Hyperion is a Coast redwood standing 381.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 18,783 ft³. Located in Redwood NP, Hyperion Valley. Hyperion is the tallest of three trillion trees on Earth. Naturalists Chris K. Atkins and Michael W. Taylor named the tree after the Greek Titan Hyperion, The High One brother of Kronos and Rhea and father of Helios. Tom McDonald Creek is also known as Fog Creek, and the surrounding valley is also known as Fog Canyon and Hyperion Valley.

locations.name: Icarus
locations.description: Icarus is a Coast redwood standing 371.19+ ft tall. Located in Redwood NP, Helios Grove. Icarus is the world's 6th tallest tree, the 4th tallest tree in Redwood National Park, and the 2nd tallest tree in Helios Grove. Naturalists Chris K. Atkins and Michael W. Taylor named the tree after Icarus, son of Daedalus, from Greek Mythology because it has a dead, sun-bleached top. As Taylor put it, "Icarus flew too close to The Sun and got burned." Elam Creek is also known as Dry Heaves Creek.

locations.name: Idril
locations.description: Idril is a Coast redwood standing 359.19+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Calf Creek Flat. Idril, also known as Alluvium, is the 3rd tallest tree on Calf Creek Flat. Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett named the tree after J. R. R. Tolkien's Idril Celebrindal, an elvish princess and mother of Eärendil the Mariner. Botanist Marie E. Antoine photographed Sillett and author Richard M. Preston near the top of Idril. Preston photographed Antoine and Sillett at the top of Idril.
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Calf Creek Flat

locations.name: Iluvatar
locations.description: Iluvatar is a Coast redwood standing 300.20+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 30,447 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove. Iluvatar, also known as Cal-Barrel Giant, is the 13th largest coast redwood, the 4th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park, and the largest tree in Atlas Grove. Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett named the tree after J. R. R. Tolkien's Eru Ilúvatar, the supreme deity of Arda.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove

locations.name: Insane
locations.description: Insane is a Coast redwood standing 341.04+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 10,570 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP. Insane, the southern tree of a pair of fused coast redwoods, is the redwood with the most-reiterated crown known. The crown contains over 700 reiterated trunk segments with a combined length of more than 0.7 mi (1.2 km).

locations.name: Ishi Giant
locations.description: Ishi Giant is a Giant sequoia standing 228.00 ft tall. Trunk volume: 19,000 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM. Before the 2015 Rough Fire, Ishi Giant was the world's 15th largest tree, the 13th largest giant redwood, and the largest tree in Kennedy Grove. The trunk lost over 20,000 ft3 (566 m3) in volume and over 27 ft (8 m) in height after it burned, and Ishi Giant no longer ranks among the 30 largest giant redwoods.

locations.name: James Irvine Giant
locations.description: James Irvine Giant is a Coast redwood standing 337.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 21,500 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Walker Grove. James Irvine Giant is the 18th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park and the 3rd largest tree in Clinton L. Walker Grove. Naturalist Michael W. Taylor named the tree James Irvine Giant after the old James Irvine Trail nearby.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Walker Grove

locations.name: Jefferson
locations.description: Jefferson is a Giant sequoia standing 258.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 29,664 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Jefferson, named to honor Thomas Jefferson, the 3rd President of the United States, is the 30th largest tree in Sequoia National Park and the 23rd largest tree in Giant Forest.

locations.name: John Muir
locations.description: John Muir is a Coast redwood standing 360.79+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 12,973 ft³. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Middle Bull Creek Flat. John Muir is the 29th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park and the 7th tallest tree on Middle Bull Creek Flat. Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett and naturalist Michael W. Taylor named the tree to honor naturalist John Muir, the father of our National Park System and patron saint of the American wilderness, whose activism led to the establishment of several national parks and to the preservation of forests in the western United States. John Muir is also the 14th fastest-growing redwood yet measured; it grew an average of 29.5 ft3 (0.84 m3) and 912 lb (414 kg) each
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Middle Bull Creek Flat

locations.name: Juggernaut
locations.description: Juggernaut is a Coast redwood standing 309.20+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 42,158 ft³. Located in Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Metcalf Grove. Juggernaut, also known as Grogan's Fault and Spartan, is the world's 9th largest tree, the 2nd largest coast redwood, the 2nd largest tree in Jedediah Smith Redwoods State Park, and the largest tree in Metcalf Grove. Juggernaut (1,359 points) is the unofficial (still unnominated) national champion coast redwood. Trunk volume estimate excludes two large basal sprouts, 500 ft3 (14 m3), which emerge on the north and east sides of the tree. Although Juggernaut and its basal sprouts may be genetically identical, experts consider them separate trees which share a common root system.
locations.location: Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Metcalf Grove

locations.name: Jupiter
locations.description: Jupiter is a Coast redwood standing 254.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 27,959 ft³. Located in Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, National Tribute Grove. Jupiter, also known as Captain Jack Sparrow, is the widest-known redwood, the coast redwood with the largest horizontal axis: 43.30 ft (13.20 m), ground perimeter: 116.00 ft (35.36 m), and footprint: 864 ft2 (80.27 m), the 17th largest coast redwood, the 7th largest tree in Jedediah Smith Redwoods State Park, and the 5th largest tree in National Tribute Grove.
locations.location: Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, National Tribute Grove

locations.name: King Arthur
locations.description: King Arthur is a Giant sequoia standing 270.30+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 40,656 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Garfield Grove. Before the 2020 Castle Fire, King Arthur, also known as California Tree and Garfield Tree, was the world's 10th largest tree, the 8th largest giant redwood, the 5th largest tree in Sequoia National Park, and the largest tree in Garfield Grove. The trunk lost significant height and volume after it burned, and, pending updated volume estimates, King Arthur likely no longer ranks among the 30 largest giant redwoods.

locations.name: Klamath Mammoth
locations.description: Klamath Mammoth is a Coast redwood standing 320.87+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 18,792 ft³. Klamath Mammoth, also known as Tree 49, is the fastest-growing tree yet measured. It gained more than 2,205 lb (1,000 kg) aboveground biomass each year, on average, during the last century and 2,811 lb (1,275 kg) aboveground biomass in 2014. Klamath Mammoth also has the largest redwood crown yet measured: 82 ft (25 m) width, 282 ft (86 m) depth, volume 1,000,676 ft3 (28,336 m3).

locations.name: Knotty Lady
locations.description: Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Walker Grove. Knotty Lady, also known as Skull Rack, was named for the numerous burls, or knots, on its trunk.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Walker Grove

locations.name: Kronos
locations.description: Kronos is a Coast redwood standing 300.52+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 11,845 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove. Kronos is the 12th largest tree in Atlas Grove. Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett named the tree after the Greek Titan Kronos, god of time, brother of Hyperion and Rhea and father of Demeter, Poseidon, and Zeus.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove

locations.name: Laurelin
locations.description: Laurelin is a Coast redwood standing 370.83+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 20,136 ft³. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Federation Grove. Laurelin, also known as Lauralyn and Federation Giant, is the world's 7th tallest tree, the 3rd tallest and 7th largest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park, and the tallest and largest tree in Federation Grove. University of California at Berkeley forestry professor Paul J. Zinke and graduate student Alan G. Stangenberger discovered Laurelin from Duckett Bluff, near Dyerville, in 1966 but did not name the tree.

locations.name: Law
locations.description: Law is a Giant sequoia standing 250.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 31,049 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Law, named to honor naturalist Michael M. Law who, with fellow naturalist Wendell D. Flint, discovered several of the largest giant redwoods, is the 25th largest tree in Sequoia National Park, and the 18th largest tree in Giant Forest.

locations.name: Lincoln
locations.description: Lincoln is a Giant sequoia standing 228.50+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 25,546 ft³. Located in Kings Canyon NP, General Grant Grove. Lincoln, named to honor Abraham Lincoln, the 16th President of the United States, is the 7th largest tree in Kings Canyon National Park, and the 5th largest tree in General Grant Grove.
locations.location: Kings Canyon NP, General Grant Grove

locations.name: Lincoln
locations.description: Lincoln is a Giant sequoia standing 255.80+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 45,030 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Lincoln, also known as Abraham Lincoln Tree, is the world's 3rd largest tree, the 3rd largest giant redwood, the 2nd largest tree in Sequoia National Park, and the 2nd largest tree in Giant Forest. The tree was named to honor Abraham Lincoln, the 16th President of the United States. Volume estimate includes main trunk only (excludes reiterated stems and branches).

locations.name: Lincoln
locations.description: Lincoln is a Giant sequoia standing 251.50+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 25,184 ft³. Located in Yosemite NP, Mariposa Grove. Lincoln, named to honor Abraham Lincoln, the 16th President of the United States, is the 8th largest tree in Yosemite National Park and the 8th largest tree in Mariposa Grove.

locations.name: Lost Monarch
locations.description: Lost Monarch is a Coast redwood standing 321.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 34,534 ft³. Located in Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Grove of Titans. Lost Monarch, also known as Súlimo, is the world's 28th largest tree, the 3rd largest coast redwood, the 3rd largest tree in Jedediah Smith Redwoods State Park, the 2nd largest tree in National Tribute Grove, and and the largest tree in the Grove of Titans.
