Although Lost Monarch (1,290 points) has been the official national champion coast redwood since 1998, Godwood Creek Giant (1,295 points) and Juggernaut (1,359 points) have since surpassed it. Trunk volume estimate excludes two large basal sprouts, 7,663 ft3 (217 m3), which emerge on the north side of the tree. These sprouts remain fused wi
locations.location: Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Grove of Titans

locations.name: Louis Agassiz
locations.description: Louis Agassiz is a Giant sequoia standing 262.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 30,580 ft³. Located in Calaveras Big Trees SP, South Grove. Louis Agassiz, named to honor Swiss-American biologist and geologist Jean Louis Rodolphe Agassiz, is the world's 29th largest tree, the 26th largest giant redwood, and the largest tree in Calaveras Big Trees State Park.
locations.location: Calaveras Big Trees SP, South Grove

locations.name: McKinley
locations.description: McKinley is a Giant sequoia standing 240.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 26,250 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. McKinley is the 28th largest tree in Giant Forest. In 1901, Sequoia National Park road foreman (later ranger, chief ranger, and superintendent) Walter Fry named the tree to honor William McKinley, the 25th President of the United States, who had been assassinated on September 14, 1901.

locations.name: Melkor
locations.description: Melkor is a Coast redwood standing 348.60+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 33,500 ft³. Located in Redwood NP, Tall Trees Grove. Melkor, also known as Fusion Giant, is the 5th largest coast redwood, the largest tree in Redwood National Park (excludes Jedediah Smith Redwoods and Prairie Creek Redwoods State Parks), and the 8th tallest tree in Tall Trees Grove. Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett renamed the tree after J. R. R. Tolkien's Melkor, the first Dark Lord of Eä. Naturalists Chris K. Atkins and Michael W. Taylor named the tree Fusion Giant.

locations.name: Mendocino
locations.description: Mendocino is a Coast redwood standing 368.37+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 8,317 ft³. Located in Montgomery Woods SNR, Montgomery Flat. Mendocino is the world's 11th tallest tree and the tallest and 5th largest tree in Montgomery Woods State Natural Reserve. Tree climber Gerald F. Beranek discovered and photographed Mendocino from the top of Montgomery Giant in June 1981 but did not name the tree. In summer 1995, naturalists Ron Hildebrant and Michael W. Taylor determined that Mendocino was the new height champion and named it after the surrounding Mendocino County. Mendocino held the title of world's tallest tree from 1995 until July 30, 2000 when naturalist Chris K. Atkins discovered Stratosphere Giant in Humboldt Redwoods S

locations.name: Methuselah
locations.description: Methuselah is a Giant sequoia standing 207.80+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 32,897 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove. Methuselah is the 8th largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument and the 4th largest tree in Mountain Home Grove. Rancher Jesse Hoskins named the tree after Methuselah, the 969-year-old oldest-living human, from the Book of Genesis in the Old Testament.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove

locations.name: Millennium
locations.description: Millennium is a Coast redwood standing 370.24+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Lower Bull Creek Flat. Millennium is the world's 9th tallest tree, the 4th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park, and the 2nd tallest tree on Lower Bull Creek Flat. Naturalist Chris K. Atkins named the tree Millennium because he and fellow naturalist Michael W. Taylor discovered the tree in January 2000 (a year before the turn of the millennium).
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Lower Bull Creek Flat

locations.name: Minaret
locations.description: Minaret is a Coast redwood standing 369.00+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Middle Bull Creek Flat. Minaret is the world's 10th tallest tree, the 5th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park, and the tallest tree on Middle Bull Creek Flat.
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Middle Bull Creek Flat

locations.name: Mojo
locations.description: Located in Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Metcalf Grove. Mojo is a coast redwood with an irregular, burl-covered base similar to that of Mutant Redwood in Praire Creek Redwoods State Park.
locations.location: Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Metcalf Grove

locations.name: Monroe
locations.description: Monroe is a Giant sequoia standing 247.80+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 40,104 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Monroe, also known as Near Auto Log (Monroe is 930 ft (283 m) north of Auto Log), is the world's 12th largest tree, the 10th largest giant redwood, the 6th largest tree in Sequoia National Park, and the 5th largest tree in Giant Forest. Volume estimate includes main trunk only (excludes basal and reiterated stems and branches). Naturalist Wendell D. Flint named the tree to honor James Monroe, 5th President of the United States.

locations.name: Montgomery Giant
locations.description: Montgomery Giant is a Coast redwood standing 360.50+ ft tall. Located in Montgomery Woods SNR, Montgomery Flat. Montgomery Giant is the largest and 4th tallest tree in Montgomery Woods State Natural Reserve. Tree climber Gerald F. Beranek discovered and photographed then-unnamed Mendocino from the top of Montgomery Giant in June 1981. Tree climber Jamie Goddard documented another climb of Montgomery Giant.

locations.name: Mother and Daughter
locations.description: Mother and Daughter is a Coast redwood standing 367.50+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Harper Flat. Mother and Daughter is is the world's 13th tallest tree, the 7th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park, and the 2nd tallest tree on Harper Flat.

locations.name: Mountain Home Monarch
locations.description: Mountain Home Monarch is a Giant sequoia standing 270.50+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 30,973 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove. Before the 2020 Castle Fire, Mountain Home Monarch was the world's 25th largest tree, the 24th largest giant redwood, the 8th largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument, and the 5th largest tree in Mountain Home Grove. The trunk lost 3,494 ft3 (99 m3) volume and 6 ft (2 m) height after it burned, and Mountain Home Monarch is now the 12th largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument and the 7th largest tree in Mountain Home Grove. The crown contains more than one-and-a-half billion leaves and eighty-six thousand cones.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove

locations.name: Muir Stub
locations.description: Muir Stub is a Giant sequoia standing 186.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 28,214 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Muir Grove. Muir Stub is the 2nd largest tree in, and the first trailside giant redwood encountered upon entering, Muir Grove.

locations.name: Mutant Redwood
locations.description: Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP. Mutant Redwood was named for its irregular, mutant form which is similar to that of Mojo in Jedediah Smith Redwoods State Park.

locations.name: Navi
locations.description: Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Goethe Grove. Navi, named after a character in the 1998 Nintendo 64 video game The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time, is a multi-stem coast redwood with a combined basal diameter of nearly 27 ft (8 m).
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Goethe Grove

locations.name: Near Ed by Ned
locations.description: Near Ed by Ned is a Giant sequoia standing 250.80+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 30,333 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Near Ed by Ned is the 26th largest tree in Sequoia National Park and the 19th largest tree in Giant Forest. Naturalists Wendell D. Flint and Jerry Latham named the tree Near Ed by Ned because it stands near Ed by Ned (Near Ed by Ned is 162 ft (49 m) north of Ed by Ned), a pair of intertwined giant redwoods named by John and James Jordan in 1906, at the southern end of Round Meadow in Giant Forest.

locations.name: Near Gothic Arch
locations.description: Near Gothic Arch is a Giant sequoia standing 227.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 33,374 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Near Gothic Arch (the tree is 50 ft (15 m) northeast of Gothic Arch), also known as Alta Twin 1, is the 30th largest giant redwood, the 15th largest tree in Sequoia National Park, and the 11th largest tree in Giant Forest.

locations.name: Near Gutless Giant
locations.description: Near Gutless Giant is a Giant sequoia standing 252.10+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 26,737 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Wheel Meadow Grove. Before it perished in the 2020 Castle Fire, Near Gutless Giant, so named because it stands near Gutless Goliath, was the largest tree in Wheel Meadow Grove.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Wheel Meadow Grove

locations.name: Near Lincoln
locations.description: Near Lincoln is a Giant sequoia standing 230.0+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 34,888 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Near Lincoln (the tree is 0.1 mi (0.2 km) west of Lincoln), also known as Rimrock and Wendell Flint Tree, is the world's 24th largest tree, the 22nd largest giant redwood, the 12th largest tree in Sequoia National Park, and the 9th largest tree in Giant Forest.

locations.name: Near McKinley
locations.description: Near McKinley is a Giant sequoia standing 217.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 28,557 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Near McKinley (the tree is 220 ft (67 m) north of McKinley) is the 25th largest tree in Giant Forest.

locations.name: Near Round Meadow
locations.description: Near Round Meadow is a Giant sequoia standing 227.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 32,527 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Near Round Meadow (the tree is 200 ft (61 m) north of Round Meadow) is the 18th largest tree in Sequoia National Park and the 14th largest tree in Giant Forest.

locations.name: Near Sherman
locations.description: Near Sherman is a Giant sequoia standing 316.60+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 17,799 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Near Sherman (the tree is 0.2 mi (0.3 km) southeast of General Sherman) is the tallest giant redwood. The crown of Near Sherman contains more than one billion leaves and over sixty-seven thousand cones.

locations.name: Near Tunnel Log
locations.description: Near Tunnel Log is a Giant sequoia standing 268.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 31,280 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Near Tunnel Log (the tree is 150 ft (46 m) northeast of Tunnel Log), also known as Moro Tunnel Loop Tree, is the 23rd largest tree in Sequoia National Park and the 17th largest tree in Giant Forest.

locations.name: Nelder
locations.description: Nelder is a Giant sequoia standing 266.20+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 34,993 ft³. Located in Sierra NF, Nelder Grove. Nelder is the world's 23rd largest tree, the 21st largest giant redwood, and the largest tree in Sierra National Forest. Naturalist Galen Clark, Judge John W. Fitzhugh, and journalist L. A. Holmes, discovered the tree during a hunting expedition on July 3, 1859. Photographer John P. Soule published a photograph of the tree in 1870.

locations.name: New Hope
locations.description: New Hope is a Coast redwood standing 356.40+ ft tall. Located in Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, National Tribute Grove. New Hope is the tallest tree in Jedediah Smith Redwoods State Park. Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett and naturalist Michael W. Taylor named the tree New Hope because, when discovered, it offered new hope of finding other redwood titans nearby. One month later, on May 11, 1998, Sillett and Taylor discovered the Grove of Titans, near Mill Creek, less than 2 mi (3 km) southeast of New Hope.
locations.location: Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, National Tribute Grove

locations.name: Newton B. Drury
locations.description: Newton B. Drury is a Coast redwood standing 275.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 29,770 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Goethe Grove. Newton B. Drury, also known as Drury Tree and Drury Giant, is the 14th largest coast redwood, the 5th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park, and the 2nd largest tree in Mary Glide Goethe Grove. Naturalist Michael W. Taylor named the to honor Newton B. Drury, former director of the National Park Service and Save the Redwoods League. Though named to honor Drury and located near a sign for Newton Bishop Drury and Aubrey Drury Memorial Grove on Newton B. Drury Scenic Parkway, the tree actually stands in Mary Glide Goethe Grove.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Goethe Grove

locations.name: Norman J. Hendry
locations.description: Norman J. Hendry is a Coast redwood standing 366.14+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 15,362 ft³. Located in Montgomery Woods SNR, Montgomery Flat. Norman J. Hendry, also known as Teepee Bell, is the world's 20th tallest tree and the 2nd tallest and 2nd largest tree in Montgomery Woods State Natural Reserve. Naturalist Michael W. Taylor named the tree to honor his step-father, Norman J. Hendry of Santa Barbara.

locations.name: Nugget
locations.description: Nugget is a Coast redwood standing 373.49+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 15,241 ft³. Located in Redwood NP, Tall Trees Grove. Nugget, also known as National Geographic Society Tree, National Geographic Tree, and Third Tallest Tree (1963), is the world's 3rd tallest tree, the 3rd tallest and 9th largest tree in Redwood National Park, and the tallest and 3rd largest tree in Tall Trees Grove. Nugget (NGT) is an acronym of National Geographic Tree. Nugget held the title of world's tallest tree from May 1991 until 1995 when Paradox was remeasured in Humboldt Redwoods State Park. Nugget is the 5th fastest-growing redwood yet measured; it gained an average of 1,336 lb (606 kg) aboveground biomass each year during a recent d

locations.name: Oliver Twist
locations.description: Oliver Twist is a Giant sequoia standing 222.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 15,160 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove. Oliver Twist, named after the main character in Charles Dickens' second novel, Oliver Twist, or The Parish Boy's Progress, was so named because the trunk has an unusual, spiral bark pattern from ground to treetop.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove

locations.name: Olympian
locations.description: Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Goethe Grove. Oympian, also known as The Emissary, is a multi-stem coast redwood with a combined basal diameter of nearly 42 ft (13 m).
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Goethe Grove

locations.name: Oregon
locations.description: Oregon is a Giant sequoia standing 268.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 35,915 ft³. Located in Kings Canyon NP, General Grant Grove. Oregon, also known as Scotland, is the world's 17th largest tree, the 15th largest giant redwood, the 3rd largest tree in Kings Canyon National Park, and the 3rd largest tree in General Grant Grove.
locations.location: Kings Canyon NP, General Grant Grove

locations.name: Orion
locations.description: Orion is a Coast redwood standing 370.34+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 14,128 ft³. Located in Redwood NP, Orion Grove. Orion, also known as TT9, is the world's 8th tallest tree, the 5th tallest and 11th largest tree in Redwood National Park, the tallest and largest tree in Orion Grove, and the tallest tree discovered by light detection and ranging (LiDAR). Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett and naturalists Chris K. Atkins and Michael W. Taylor named the tree after Orion, the hunter in Greek mythology, whom the Greek Olympian Zeus placed among the stars as a constellation. Elam Creek is also known as Dry Heaves Creek.

locations.name: Outlier
locations.description: Outlier is a Coast redwood standing 360.50+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 15,617 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Irvine Grove. Outlier, also known as TT2, is the tallest and 27th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park and the 5th tallest tree detected by light detection and ranging (LiDAR). It grew an average of 18.3 ft3 (0.52 m3) and 704 lb (319 kg) each year over a recent decade.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Irvine Grove

locations.name: Packsaddle Giant
locations.description: Packsaddle Giant is a Giant sequoia standing 218.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 32,156 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Packsaddle Grove. Before it perished in the 2021 Windy Fire, Packsaddle Giant was the 10th largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument and the largest tree in Packsaddle Grove.

locations.name: Paradox
locations.description: Paradox is a Coast redwood standing 371.78+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 11,688 ft³. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Lower Bull Creek Flat. Paradox is the world's 5th tallest tree, the 2nd tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park, and the tallest tree on Lower Bull Creek Flat. University of California at Berkeley forestry professor Paul J. Zinke and graduate student Alan G. Stangenberger discovered Paradox from Duckett Bluff, near Dyerville, in 1966 but did not name the tree. In February 1993, Zinke told naturalist Michael W. Taylor where to search for his Three Peas in a Pod (then-unnamed Laurelin, Paradox, and Telperion) which, Zinke suspected, were the world's tallest trees. When Taylor found Paradox later, he named it Para
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Lower Bull Creek Flat

locations.name: Passageway
locations.description: Passageway is a Giant sequoia standing 254.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 32,060 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Passageway, named for the large passageway through the western side of the trunk, is the 20th largest tree in Sequoia National Park and the 15th largest tree in Giant Forest.

locations.name: Patriarch
locations.description: Patriarch is a Giant sequoia standing 176.40+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 30,020 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM. Beofore it perished in the 2020 Castle Fire, Patriarch was the 17th largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument and the largest tree in McIntyre Grove.

locations.name: Paul Zahl
locations.description: Paul Zahl is a Coast redwood standing 366.40+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 9,887 ft³. Located in Redwood NP, Tall Trees Grove. Paul Zahl, also known as Twin Tower, is the world's 17th tallest tree, the 6th tallest tree in Redwood National Park, and the 2nd tallest tree in Tall Trees Grove. The tree was named to honor National Geographic Society senior scientist Paul A. Zahl who, in 1963, discovered Howard Libbey, then the world's tallest tree, in Tall Trees Grove. Zahl's discovery, featured on the cover of the July 1964 issue of National Geographic magazine and in an article in the same issue entitled Finding the Mt. Everest of All Living Things, led to the creation of Redwood National Park in 1968.

locations.name: Paul Zinke
locations.description: Paul Zinke is a Coast redwood standing 365.39+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 7,685 ft³. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Lower Bull Creek Flat. Paul Zinke is the world's 21st tallest tree, the 12th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park, and the 3rd tallest tree on Lower Bull Creek Flat. Naturalist Michael W. Taylor named the tree to honor University of California at Berkeley forestry professor Paul J. Zinke who discovered Dyerville Giant, Laurelin, Paradox, and Telperion.
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Lower Bull Creek Flat

locations.name: Pinnacle
locations.description: Pinnacle is a Coast redwood standing 364.00+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Lower Bull Creek Flat. Pinnacle is the world's 28th tallest tree, the 19th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park, and the 4th tallest tree in Lower Bull Creek Flat.
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Lower Bull Creek Flat

locations.name: Pipe Dream
locations.description: Pipe Dream is a Coast redwood standing 367.10+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 14,817 ft³. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Middle Bull Creek Flat. Pipe Dream is the world's 14th tallest tree, the 8th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park, and the 2nd tallest tree on Middle Bull Creek Flat. The area surrounding Pipe Dream is also known as Patriarch Forest and Patriarch Grove. Pipe Dream is also the 21st fastest-growing redwood yet measured; it grew an average of 23.0 ft3 (0.65 m3) and 578 lb (262 kg) each year over a recent decade.
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Middle Bull Creek Flat

locations.name: Pleiades 1
locations.description: Pleiades 1 is a Coast redwood standing 309.71+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 16,103 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove. Pleiades 1 is the 25th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park and the 9th largest tree in Atlas Grove. Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett named the tree cluster after The Pleiades, seven daughters of the Greek Titan Atlas, whom the Greek Olympian Zeus placed among the stars as a constellation.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove

locations.name: Pleiades 2
locations.description: Pleiades 2 is a Coast redwood standing 296.92+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 20,822 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove. Pleiades 2 is the 21st largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park and the 5th largest tree in Atlas Grove. Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett named the tree cluster after The Pleiades, seven daughters of the Greek Titan Atlas, whom the Greek Olympian Zeus placed among the stars as a constellation.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove

locations.name: Poseidon
locations.description: Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove. Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett named the tree after the Greek Olympian Poseidon, god of the sea, son of Kronos and Rhea and brother of Demeter and Zeus. Author Richard M. Preston published photographs of the uppermost trunks of Poseidon and a large, heart-shaped fern mat, known as The Heart of Poseidon, which grows within the canopy.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove

locations.name: President
locations.description: President is a Giant sequoia standing 246.62+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 44,567 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. President, also known as Warren Harding Tree, is the world's 5th largest tree, the 4th largest giant redwood, the 3rd largest tree in Sequoia National Park, the 3rd largest tree in Giant Forest, and the oldest living redwood.

locations.name: Proclamation
locations.description: Proclamation is a Giant sequoia standing 245.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 25,109 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Long Meadow Grove. On April 15, 2000, during a ceremony beside the tree, President William J. Clinton signed Proclamation 7295 to establish Giant Sequoia National Monument. His proclamation protected 41 federally-owned giant redwood groves, several limestone caverns, paleontological resources, Native American archaeological sites, and historic remnants of early Euro-American settlement within 513 mi2 (1329 km2) of Sequoia National Forest.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Long Meadow Grove

locations.name: Prometheus
locations.description: Prometheus is a Coast redwood standing 319.55+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 20,702 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove. Prometheus is the 22nd largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park, and the 6th largest tree in Atlas Grove. Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett named the tree after the Greek Titan Prometheus, god of foresight, brother of Atlas and Epimetheus.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove

locations.name: Pyramid Giant
locations.description: Pyramid Giant is a Coast redwood standing 364.70+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Harper Flat. Pyramid Giant is the world's 24th tallest tree, the 15th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park, and the 4th tallest tree on Harper Flat.

locations.name: Randy Stoltmann
locations.description: Randy Stoltmann is a Coast redwood standing 364.70+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Harper Flat. Randy Stoltmann is the world's 25th tallest tree, the 16th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park and the 5th tallest tree on Harper Flat. Naturalist Michael W. Taylor named the tree to honor fellow naturalist Randy Stoltmann who died unexpectedly on May 22, 1994 while mountaineering in British Columbia, Canada.

locations.name: Red Chief
locations.description: Red Chief is a Giant sequoia standing 220.60+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 28,723 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Long Meadow Grove. Red Chief, also known as Deer Fly and Long Meadow Tree, is the 19th largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument and the largest tree in Long Meadow Grove.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Long Meadow Grove

locations.name: Red Hill Giant
locations.description: Red Hill Giant is a Giant sequoia standing 268.37+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 33,902 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM. Before the 2021 Windy Fire, Red Hill Giant was the 28th largest giant redwood, the 7th largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument, and the largest tree in Red Hill Grove. The crown contained more than one-and-a-half billion leaves and fifty-one thousand cones. The trunk lost significant height and volume after it burned, and, pending updated volume estimates, Red Hill Giant likely no longer ranks among the 30 largest giant redwoods.

locations.name: Redwood Creek Giant
locations.description: Redwood Creek Giant is a Coast redwood standing 360.20+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 26,299 ft³. Located in Redwood NP, Tall Trees Grove. Redwood Creek Giant is the 22nd largest coast redwood, the 3rd largest and 13th tallest tree in Redwood National Park, and the 2nd largest and 4th tallest tree in Tall Trees Grove. The main trunk remains over 10 ft (3 m) wide 213 ft (65 m) above ground, and the crown, one of the most-complex known, contains 148 reiterated trunks.

locations.name: Rhea
locations.description: Rhea is a Coast redwood standing 313.32+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 12,672 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove. Rhea is the 30th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park and the 11th largest tree in Atlas Grove. Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett named the tree after the Greek Titan Rhea, the mother of gods, sister of Hyperion and Kronos and mother of Demeter, Poseidon, and Zeus.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove

locations.name: Robert E. Lee
locations.description: Robert E. Lee is a Giant sequoia standing 256.56+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 40,435 ft³. Located in Kings Canyon NP, General Grant Grove. Robert E. Lee is the world's 11th largest tree, the 9th largest giant redwood, the 2nd largest tree in Kings Canyon National Park, and the 2nd largest tree in General Grant Grove. In 1875, Confederate Army Lieutenant Richard Field named the tree to honor Confederate Army General Robert E. Lee. The crown contains nearly one-and-a-half billion leaves and over twenty-three thousand cones.
locations.location: Kings Canyon NP, General Grant Grove

locations.name: Rockefeller
locations.description: Rockefeller is a Coast redwood standing 366.50+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 20,000 ft³. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Upper Bull Creek Flat. Rockefeller, also known as Tall Tree, Tallest Tree, Fourth Tallest Tree (1963), and Jerry Partain Tree, is the world's 16th tallest tree, the 10th tallest and 8th largest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park, and the tallest and 2nd largest tree on Upper Bull Creek Flat. Rockefeller was named to honor John D. Rockefeller Jr. who, between 1926 and 1929, donated $2,000,000 to Save the Redwoods League to purchase Dyerville Flat from the Pacific Lumber Company. In 1931, this 14.6 mi2 (37.8 km2) tract of old-growth redwood forest, since renamed Rockefeller Forest, became the core of Humboldt Redwoo
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Upper Bull Creek Flat

locations.name: Rocket Top
locations.description: Rocket Top is a Coast redwood standing 364.50+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Middle Bull Creek Flat. Rocket top is the world's 26th tallest tree, the 17th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park, and the 4th tallest tree on Middle Bull Creek Flat.
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Middle Bull Creek Flat

locations.name: Rockview
locations.description: Rockview is a Coast redwood standing 361.81+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Upper Bull Creek Flat. Rockview is the 24th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park and the 3rd tallest tree on Upper Bull Creek Flat. Naturalist Michael W. Taylor named the tree Rockview because it grows directly across Bull Creek from Rockefeller.
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Upper Bull Creek Flat

locations.name: Room
locations.description: Room is a Giant sequoia standing 240.5 ft tall. Trunk volume: 28,346 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. In 1901, Sequoia National Park concessionaire John Broder named the tree for the large, 40 ft (12 m) tall and 15 ft (5 m) wide, fire-hollowed room within the trunk. Ladders, once installed inside and outside the tree against the northeast side of the trunk, enabled visitors to climb through a narrow window, located 6 ft (3 m) above ground, to descend into the room, and to leave through an opening in the south side of the trunk. Sometime before 1975, the National Park Service removed the ladders for safety reasons.

locations.name: Roosevelt
locations.description: Roosevelt is a Giant sequoia standing 260.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 35,013 ft³. Located in Kings Canyon NP, Redwood Mountain Grove. Before the 2021 KNP Complex Fire, Roosevelt, also known as False Hart, was the world's 22nd largest tree, the 20th largest giant redwood, the 4th largest tree in Kings Canyon National Park, and the largest tree in Redwood Mountain Grove. The tree was named to honor Theodore Roosevelt, 26th President of the United States. The trunk lost significant height and volume after it burned, and, pending updated volume estimates, Roosevelt likely no longer ranks among the 30 largest giant redwoods.
locations.location: Kings Canyon NP, Redwood Mountain Grove

locations.name: Roosevelt
locations.description: Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. In 1901, Sequoia National Park acting superintendent Captain Lincoln C. Andrews named the tree to honor Theodore Roosevelt, the 26th President of the United States. In his report to the Secretary of the Interior, dated October 13, 1901, Andrews wrote, "Between the road and Moro Rock stands a lone Sequoia, considered the most perfect specimen, though not so large, which I have named for His Excellency Theodore Roosevelt, President of the United States."

locations.name: Rosebark
locations.description: Rosebark is a Coast redwood standing 361.38+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Middle Bull Creek Flat. Rosebark is the 26th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park and the 6th tallest tree on Middle Bull Creek Flat.
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Middle Bull Creek Flat

locations.name: Sacagawea
locations.description: Sacagawea is a Coast redwood standing 321.19+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 23,876 ft³. Located in Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Grove of Titans. Sacagawea is the 28th largest coast redwood, the 8th largest tree in Jedediah Smith Redwoods State Park, the 6th largest tree in National Tribute Grove, and the 4th largest tree in the Grove of Titans. Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett and naturalist Michael W. Taylor named the tree to honor Lemhi Shoshone Native American Sacagawea who served as both guide and interpreter for the Lewis and Clark Expedition from 1805 to 1806.
locations.location: Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Grove of Titans

locations.name: Screaming Titans
locations.description: Screaming Titans is a Coast redwood standing 323.00+ ft tall. Located in Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Grove of Titans. Screaming Titans is a large, multi-stem coast redwood: 2 two fused trees with a combined width of 25.6 ft (7.8 m). Cal Poly Humboldt forestry professor Stephen C. Sillett named the tree Screaming Titans because naturalist Michael W. Taylor screamed when he first spotted it in the Grove of Titans on May 11, 1998.
locations.location: Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Grove of Titans

locations.name: Sentinel
locations.description: Sentinel is a Giant sequoia standing 257.60+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 27,900 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Sentinel, which stands directly in front of Giant Forest Muesuem, is the 26th largest tree in Giant Forest.

locations.name: Shrine
locations.description: Shrine is a Coast redwood standing 96.80 ft tall. Shrine Drive Thru Tree, also known as Doust Tree, Redwood Shrine Tree, and Shrine of the Redwoods, is the third coast redwood tunnel tree. Located at 13078 California State Route 254 (Avenue of the Giants) in Myers Flat, California 3.9 mi (6.3 km) south of the Humboldt Redwoods State Park visitor center, Shrine is a chimney tree whose trunk was hollowed by fire (perhaps the result of a lightning strike) more than a century ago. Loggers reportedly spared the tree because they suspected it had little, if any, merchantable timber.

locations.name: Sir Isaac Newton
locations.description: Sir Isaac Newton is a Coast redwood standing 299.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 33,192 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Goethe Grove. Sir Isaac Newton, also known as Newton Tree, is the 6th largest coast redwood and the largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park. Naturalist Michael W. Taylor named the tree Newton after nearby Newton B. Drury Scenic Parkway but, at fellow naturalist Ronald W. Hildebrant's urging, renamed it Sir Isaac Newton to avoid confusion with Newton B. Drury Tree. Once 311 ft (95 m) tall, Sir Isaac Newton lost height when part of its dead top broke off in 1991.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Goethe Grove

locations.name: Solstice
locations.description: Solstice is a Coast redwood standing 353.40+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP. Solstice is the tallest tree in Walter A. and Carmen Starr Grove. Naturalist Michael W. Taylor named the tree Solstice because he first measured the tree on the winter solstice (December 21, 2017).

locations.name: South Fork
locations.description: South Fork is a Coast redwood standing 362.70+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Founders Grove. South Fork, also known as UT63, is the 21st tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park, and the tallest tree in Founders Grove. The tree was named after the nearby South Fork Eel River.

locations.name: Spartacus
locations.description: Spartacus is a Coast redwood standing 315.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 26,000 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP. Spartacus, also known as Sneaky Pete, is the 23rd largest coast redwood, the 11th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park, and the largest tree in John and Edgar H. Vance and Edgar Vance Morgan Grove.

locations.name: Stagg
locations.description: Stagg is a Giant sequoia standing 243.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 44,100 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Alder Creek Grove. Stagg, originally known as Day Tree, is the world's 6th largest tree, the 5th largest giant redwood, and the largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument. On August 16, 1960, the Rouch Family, which owned the tree before Save the Redwoods League acquired Alder Creek Grove on December 30, 2019, dedicated the tree to honor Amos Alonzo Stagg, a pioneering football coach at the University of the Pacific and a College Football Hall of Fame inductee, whose innovations included the Notre Dame Box and the Statue of Liberty play.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Alder Creek Grove

locations.name: Stout
locations.description: Stout is a Coast redwood standing 325.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 21,966 ft³. Located in Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Stout Grove. Stout Tree, also known as Big Tree (not to be confused with Big Tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park) is the 9th largest tree in Jedediah Smith Redwoods State Park and the largest tree in Stout Grove. In 1929, Clara W. Stout, widow of lumberman Frank D. Stout, donated a 0.07 mi2 (0.18 km2) tract of old-growth redwood forest to Save the Redwoods League in memory of her late husband. Stout Grove became the first dedicated grove in Jedediah Smith Redwoods State Park.
locations.location: Jedediah Smith Redwoods SP, Stout Grove

locations.name: Stratosphere Giant
locations.description: Stratosphere Giant is a Coast redwood standing 372.44+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 22,071 ft³. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Calf Creek Flat. Stratosphere Giant is the world's 4th tallest tree, the tallest and 4th largest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park, and the tallest and largest tree on Calf Creek Flat. It held the title of world's tallest tree from July 30, 2000 until July 1, 2006 when naturalists Chris K. Atkins and Michael W. Taylor discovered Helios in Redwood National Park.
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Calf Creek Flat

locations.name: Summit Road
locations.description: Summit Road is a Giant sequoia standing 218.50+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 31,762 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove. Before the 2020 Castle Fire, Summit Road, named for the road nearby, was the world's 17th largest tree, the 16th largest giant redwood, the 5th largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument, and the 2nd largest tree in Mountain Home Grove. The trunk lost 4,838 ft3 (137 m3) volume and 25 ft (8 m) height after it burned, and Summit Road is now the 10th largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument and the 5th largest tree in Mountain Home Grove.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove

locations.name: Super Colossus
locations.description: Super Colossus is a Coast redwood standing 143.00+ ft tall. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Volkmann Grove. Super Colossus is an usual redwood with a large, fire-hollowed burl which engulfs two smaller redwood trunks. With the hollow burl included, Super Colossus ranks as the 2nd widest coast redwood.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Volkmann Grove

locations.name: Susan B. Anthony
locations.description: Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. On June 26, 1938, Sequoia National Park superintendent Colonel John R. White dedicated the tree to honor social reformer and women's rights activist Susan B. Anthony who had died 32 years earlier.

locations.name: Telescope
locations.description: Telescope is a Giant sequoia standing 188.00+ ft tall. Located in Yosemite NP, Mariposa Grove. Telescope is a giant redwood whose fire-hollowed trunk provides a telescopic view of the sky.

locations.name: Terex Titan
locations.description: Terex Titan is a Coast redwood standing 270.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 30,869 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP. Terex Titan, also known as Terex Giant, is the 10th largest coast redwood, the 3rd largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park, and the largest tree in Edward C. Wagner Grove. Naturalist Ronald W. Hildebrant named the tree after the General Motors Terex 33-19 Titan haul truck which was, at that time, the world's largest truck. Although the main trunk broke off 213 ft (65 m) above ground long ago, reiterated trunks have since restored most of the original canopy. An 8.2 ft (2.5 m) tall, 1.7 in (4.2 cm) wide Sitka spruce (Picea sitchensis) grows atop one reiterated trunk 174 ft (53 m) abov

locations.name: Texas
locations.description: Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Whitehouse Grove. Texas, also known as Mata Hari, has a Texas-shaped burn scar on the north side of the trunk.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Whitehouse Grove

locations.name: Tharp's Log
locations.description: Tharp's Log is a Giant sequoia standing 70.00 ft tall. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Tharp's Log, also known as Tharp Cabin and Wolverton Log, is a fallen, fire-hollowed, giant redwood log at the northern edge of Log Meadow in Giant Forest and the oldest remaining pioneer cabin in Sequoia National Park. When this tree fell more than 300 years ago, the trunk split into two sections; the lower, 70 ft (21 m)-long section lies north of Tharp's Log Trail while the upper, 50 ft (15 m)-long section lies just south of the trail.

locations.name: The Cloister
locations.description: Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. The Cloister is a cluster of four giant redwoods, located near Lincoln in Giant Forest, which surrounds a group of white fir trees. Sequoia National Park superintendent Colonel John R. White named this tree cluster in 1920.

locations.name: The House
locations.description: Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. The House is a cluster of giant redwoods located near President in Giant Forest. In July 1922, at the suggestion of chief ranger Guy Hopping, Sequoia National Park superintendent Colonel John R. White named this tree cluster to honor the United States House of Representatives. Together, The House and The Senate form The Congress Group.

locations.name: The Senate
locations.description: Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. The Senate is a cluster of giant redwoods located near President in Giant Forest. In July 1922, at the suggestion of chief ranger Guy Hopping, Sequoia National Park superintendent Colonel John R. White named this tree cluster to honor the United States Senate. Together, The House and The Senate form The Congress Group.

locations.name: Three Fingered Jack
locations.description: Three Fingered Jack is a Giant sequoia standing 196.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 28,467 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove. Before the 2020 Castle Fire, Three Fingered Jack was the 16th largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument, and the 8th largest tree in Mountain Home Grove. The trunk lost 1,651 ft3 (47 m3) volume and 44 ft (13 m) height after it burned, and Three Fingered Jack is now the 21st largest tree in Giant Sequoia National Monument and the 12th largest tree in Mountain Home Grove.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Mountain Home Grove

locations.name: Titanic Cathedral
locations.description: Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP. Titanic Cathedral is a cathedral tree (also known as a fairy ring) which formed after the main trunk was damaged and several basal sprouts grew in a ringlike pattern around it.

locations.name: Tom McDonald
locations.description: Tom McDonald is a Coast redwood standing 351.12+ ft tall. Located in Redwood NP, Hyperion Valley. Tom McDonald, also known as The Beast, is the 3rd tallest tree in Hyperion Valley. National Geographic photographer Michael K. Nichols photographed Tom McDonald from Crown Jewel. Videographer Steven A. Hall Jr. documented a hike up Tom McDonald Creek to Crown Jewel which stands beside Tom McDonald.

locations.name: Tour Thru
locations.description: Tour Thru is a Coast redwood standing 167.54+ ft tall. Tour Thru Tree is the fourth coast redwood tunnel tree. Located at 430 California State Route 169 (Klamath Glen Road) in Klamath, California 21 mi (34 km) south of Crescent City, Tour Thru and surrounding trees were spared when the area was logged in 1967.

locations.name: Tranquility
locations.description: Tranquility is a Coast redwood standing 363.32+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 13,556 ft³. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Lower Bull Creek Flat. Tranquility is the 20th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park and the 5th tallest tree on Lower Bull Creek Flat.
locations.location: Humboldt Redwoods SP, Lower Bull Creek Flat

locations.name: Tunnel
locations.description: Tunnel is a Giant sequoia standing 274.00 ft tall. Trunk volume: 27,436 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Atwell Mill Grove. Tunnel Tree has an enormous, 57 ft (17 m) wide, flared base through which fire once burned a tunnel. Naturalist Wendell D. Flint, who discovered the tree, claimed that "three compact cars could be driven abreast halfway through it."

locations.name: Tunnel Log
locations.description: Tunnel Log is a Giant sequoia standing 275.00 ft tall. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Tunnel Log, known originally as Colonel Tunnel Log, was named for the large tunnel cut through its trunk during the summer of 1938. When the previously-unnamed giant redwood fell across Crescent Meadow Road on December 4, 1937, Roy Devoe, a surveyor for the Civilian Conservation Corps at Camp Wolverton, suggested cutting a tunnel through the fallen giant to clear the road rather than cutting apart and removing the tree. After Sequoia National Park superintendent Colonel John R. White approved his plan, Devoe and his crew used adzes and torches to carve the 17.0 ft (5.2 m) wide and 8.0 ft (2.4

locations.name: Valentine
locations.description: Valentine is a Coast redwood standing 364.30+ ft tall. Located in Humboldt Redwoods SP, Harper Flat. Valentine is the world's 27th tallest tree, the 18th tallest tree in Humboldt Redwoods State Park, and the 6th tallest tree on Harper Flat.

locations.name: Washington
locations.description: Washington is a Giant sequoia standing 115.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 23,818 ft³. Located in Sequoia NP, Giant Forest. Once considered the world's 2nd largest tree, Washington, the 5th oldest living redwood, was named to honor George Washington, the 1st President of the United States. In 1999, Washington was 253.61 ft (77.30 m) tall and 27.92 ft (8.51 m) wide and contained 44,108 ft3 (1,249 m3) of wood. The crown had 46 reiterated trunks, nearly two billion leaves, and sixty-three thousand cones. The main trunk contained a hollow pit, accessible via an opening located 190 ft (58 m) above ground, approximately 6-9 ft (2-3 m) wide and 115 ft (35 m) deep. At that time, Washington was actually the world's 6th larg

locations.name: Washington
locations.description: Washington is a Giant sequoia standing 233.76+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 33,372 ft³. Located in Yosemite NP, Mariposa Grove. Washington, named to honor George Washington, the 1st President of the United States, is the largest tree in Yosemite National Park. The crown of Washington contains more than one billion leaves and forty-three thousand cones. A 128-year-old ponderosa pine (Pinus ponderosa) grows atop a limb within the crown.

locations.name: Waterfall
locations.description: Waterfall is a Giant sequoia standing 225.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 25,000 ft³. Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Alder Creek Grove. Waterfall, also known as Big Base Tree, has the largest redwood ground perimeter (140.10 ft or 42.70 m) and horizontal axis (57.00 ft or 17.37 m). The steep (greater than 45 degree or 100%) slope and large buttresses on the north/downhill side of the trunk combine to create the tree's extraordinary ground perimeter. Although the trunk collapsed after it burned during the 2020 Castle Fire, the base of the tree remains intact.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Alder Creek Grove

locations.name: West Ridge Giant
locations.description: West Ridge Giant is a Coast redwood standing 244.00+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 29,541 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Walker Grove. West Ridge Giant is the 15th largest coast redwood, the 6th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park, and the largest tree in Clinton L. Walker Grove. Naturalist Michael Taylor named the tree after the West Ridge Trail nearby.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Walker Grove

locations.name: Whitehouse Giant
locations.description: Whitehouse Giant, a large coast redwood located in George Bruen Whitehouse Grove in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park, appears in a San Francisco Chronicle article entitled "Should we hide the locations of Earth's greatest trees?"

locations.name: Window
locations.description: Located in Giant Sequoia NM, Alder Creek Grove. Window was a giant redwood with an enormous hole, or window, through the trunk formed by lightning, fire, and decay. The trunk collapsed after it burned in the 2020 Castle Fire.
locations.location: Giant Sequoia NM, Alder Creek Grove

locations.name: Yale
locations.description: Yale is a Giant sequoia standing 267.50+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 32,249 ft³. Located in Yosemite NP, Mariposa Grove. Yale, named to honor Yale University and also known as Upper Grove Giant, is the 2nd largest tree in Yosemite National Park and the 2nd largest tree in Mariposa Grove.

locations.name: Yosemite Sam
locations.description: Yosemite Sam is a Giant sequoia standing 291.67+ ft tall. Located in Yosemite NP, Mariposa Grove. Yosemite Sam is the tallest tree in Yosemite National Park.

locations.name: Zeus
locations.description: Zeus is a Coast redwood standing 334.22+ ft tall. Trunk volume: 23,073 ft³. Located in Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove. Zeus is the 16th largest tree in Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park, and the tallest and 3rd largest tree in Atlas Grove. Zeus is also the 4th fastest-growing redwood yet measured; it gained an average of 1,501 lb (681 kg) aboveground biomass each year during a recent decade.
locations.location: Prairie Creek Redwoods SP, Atlas Grove

locations.name: Colonel Armstrong Tree
locations.description: The Colonel Armstrong Tree is a coast redwood estimated to be over 1,400 years old, standing 308 feet tall and 14.6 feet in diameter. It is the centerpiece of Armstrong Redwoods State Natural Reserve near Guerneville in Sonoma County. The grove was protected in the 1870s by lumberman Colonel James Armstrong, who recognized its value and set it aside — one of the earliest private conservation efforts for redwoods.
locations.location: Armstrong Redwoods State Natural Reserve, Guerneville, Sonoma County

locations.name: Pioneer Cabin Tree (fallen 2017)
locations.description: The Pioneer Cabin Tree was a giant sequoia in Calaveras Big Trees State Park with a tunnel carved through its base in the 1880s for tourist stagecoaches. For over a century it was a beloved California icon. On January 8, 2017, after a powerful atmospheric river storm saturated the soil, the tree fell — ending its 1,000+ year life. The stump and fallen log remain at the site as a natural memorial.
locations.location: Calaveras Big Trees State Park, Calaveras County

locations.name: Sentinel Tree
locations.description: The Sentinel Tree is a massive ponderosa pine that stands as a solitary landmark on the floor of Yosemite Valley near Yosemite Village. Its commanding silhouette against El Capitan and Half Dome has made it one of the most photographed trees in the park. Valley ponderosa pines can live 300–600 years; the Sentinel is an example of the old-growth forest that once filled the valley floor before early visitor-era landscape management.
locations.location: Yosemite Valley, Yosemite National Park, Mariposa County

locations.name: Berkeley Blue Gum Forest
locations.description: Strawberry Canyon above UC Berkeley is home to a dramatic blue gum eucalyptus forest planted in the early 1900s when eucalyptus was promoted as a fast-growing timber crop. The trees grew to 200+ feet, creating a cathedral-like grove beloved by Bay Area hikers. The grove is ecologically controversial — blue gums are non-native and displace native habitat — but culturally significant and visually stunning, especially in morning fog.
locations.location: Strawberry Canyon, UC Berkeley Hills, Alameda County

locations.name: Moreton Bay Fig (Golden Gate Park)
locations.description: This Moreton Bay fig (Ficus macrophylla) near the Music Concourse in Golden Gate Park has a canopy spread exceeding 150 feet — so wide that its branches touch the ground and re-root. Planted in the 1800s, it is one of San Francisco's most spectacular trees. The buttressed root system creates a surreal landscape that invites exploration. It is a designated San Francisco landmark.

locations.name: Moreton Bay Fig (Santa Barbara Amtrak)
locations.description: This enormous Moreton Bay fig next to the Santa Barbara Amtrak station is one of the largest trees in North America by canopy spread, with branches extending nearly 170 feet across. Planted in 1877 from an Australian seedling, it now stands 80 feet tall with a circumference of over 38 feet. It has been a California State Historic Landmark since 1970. Train passengers disembark directly beneath its shade.
locations.location: Santa Barbara Amtrak Station, Chapala & Mason, Santa Barbara

locations.name: Pechanga Great Oak
locations.description: The Pechanga Great Oak is a coast live oak (Quercus agrifolia) estimated to be over 1,000 years old, making it one of the oldest oaks in North America. It stands on the Pechanga Band of Luiseño Indians' reservation near Temecula. The tribe considers the tree sacred and has long protected it. When a casino was proposed nearby in the 1990s, the tribe specifically redesigned the project to preserve the oak's root zone.
locations.location: Pechanga Reservation, Temecula area, Riverside County

locations.name: Murrietta Falls
locations.description: A 100-foot waterfall deep in Ohlone Regional Wilderness, accessible only via the challenging Ohlone Wilderness Trail. Best flow in winter and spring.

locations.name: Phantom Falls
locations.description: A striking 164-foot seasonal waterfall on North Table Mountain near Oroville. The mesa's flat volcanic plateau creates dramatic cascades after winter rains — truly a 'phantom' that vanishes by late spring.
locations.location: North Table Mountain Ecological Reserve, Butte County

locations.name: Feather Falls
locations.description: At 410 feet, Feather Falls is one of the highest free-leaping waterfalls in the continental United States. Located in Plumas National Forest, a 9.5-mile loop trail leads to a breathtaking overlook.

locations.name: Bassi Falls
locations.description: A 109-foot cascade along Bassi Creek in El Dorado National Forest, accessed via a short trail off Ice House Road. The falls drop into a granite bowl popular for swimming.
locations.location: El Dorado National Forest, El Dorado County

locations.name: Bridal Veil Falls
locations.description: An 81-foot waterfall near the Fallen Leaf Lake and Emerald Bay area of Lake Tahoe. Not to be confused with the more famous Yosemite namesake — this Sierra Nevada gem rewards those who seek it out.
locations.location: Fallen Leaf Lake area, El Dorado County

locations.name: Eagle Falls
locations.description: A 160-foot waterfall plunging toward Emerald Bay at Lake Tahoe, one of the most photographed falls in the Sierra. A short but steep hike from Hwy 89 leads to the falls and upper Eagle Lake.
locations.location: Emerald Bay State Park, El Dorado County

locations.name: Glen Alpine Falls
locations.description: A 73-foot waterfall on Glen Alpine Creek near Fallen Leaf Lake. Best seen from the road or a brief trail walk, surrounded by historic resort buildings from the early 1900s.
locations.location: Fallen Leaf Lake area, El Dorado County

locations.name: Horsetail Falls
locations.description: A spectacular 500-foot cascade on Pyramid Creek near Twin Bridges, visible from Hwy 50 and reached via a rough scramble through granite. One of the most dramatic waterfalls in the Lake Tahoe area.

locations.name: Modjeska Falls
locations.description: A 51-foot waterfall in the upper Glen Alpine area near Fallen Leaf Lake, named after 19th-century actress Helena Modjeska who visited the region.
locations.location: Fallen Leaf Lake area, El Dorado County

locations.name: Pyramid Creek Cascades
locations.description: A series of 75-foot cascades along Pyramid Creek near Horsetail Falls at Twin Bridges. The granite slabs and pools make for excellent scrambling and swimming.

locations.name: Dinkey Creek Falls
locations.description: A 400-foot cascade on Dinkey Creek in Sierra National Forest. The Dinkey Creek area features granite domes, meadows, and multiple swimming holes surrounding this impressive falls.

locations.name: Garlic Falls
locations.description: An 820-foot waterfall in the remote backcountry of Kings Canyon. One of the lesser-known giants of the Sierra, requiring a multi-day backpacking trip to reach.
locations.location: Kings Canyon National Park backcountry, Fresno County

locations.name: Grizzly Falls
locations.description: A 150-foot roadside waterfall in Kings Canyon National Park, one of the most accessible in the park. Located along Hwy 180, the falls are just steps from the road.
locations.location: Kings Canyon National Park, Fresno County

locations.name: Mist Falls
locations.description: A 50-foot cascade at the end of the popular Mist Falls Trail from Roads End in Kings Canyon. One of the largest cascades in the Sierra Nevada by volume, with a thundering roar in spring.
locations.location: Kings Canyon National Park, Fresno County

locations.name: Roaring River Falls
locations.description: A 40-foot waterfall that bursts through a narrow granite slot into a pool in Kings Canyon National Park. A short quarter-mile walk from the road makes it very accessible.
locations.location: Kings Canyon National Park, Fresno County

locations.name: Darwin Falls
locations.description: An 80-foot oasis waterfall in Death Valley National Park, fed by a reliable spring that creates a lush riparian canyon in the heart of the desert. A surprising gem in an otherwise arid landscape.
locations.location: Death Valley National Park, Inyo County

locations.name: Portal Falls
locations.description: A 150-foot waterfall near Whitney Portal, gateway to the highest peak in the contiguous US. The falls tumble through granite above the Mount Whitney trailhead.

locations.name: Corlieu Falls
locations.description: An 80-foot cascade on the South Fork of the San Joaquin River, accessible from Hwy 41 near Fish Camp. A beautiful, often overlooked waterfall on the southern route to Yosemite.

locations.name: Rainbow Falls
locations.description: A 101-foot waterfall at Devil's Postpile National Monument near Mammoth. The San Joaquin River plunges over a rhyolite cliff — rainbows form in the afternoon mist, giving the falls their name.
locations.location: Devil's Postpile National Monument, Madera County

locations.name: Reconnaissance Creek Falls
locations.description: A massive 900-foot waterfall in Sierra National Forest, one of the tallest in the Sierra Nevada. Remote and rarely visited, requiring a lengthy backcountry approach.

locations.name: Alamere Falls
locations.description: A rare 60-foot tidefall that drops directly onto the beach at Point Reyes National Seashore. One of only a handful of tidefalls on the US Pacific Coast. Reach via the Coast Trail from Palomarin Trailhead.
locations.location: Point Reyes National Seashore, Marin County

locations.name: Cascade Falls
locations.description: A seasonal cascade in Baltimore Canyon Open Space Preserve in Marin County. Best seen during the rainy season, the falls flow through a lush redwood and bay laurel canyon.
locations.location: Baltimore Canyon Open Space, Marin County

locations.name: Cataract Falls
locations.description: A series of cascades tumbling down the flanks of Mount Tamalpais along Cataract Creek. The trail offers multiple viewpoints as it climbs through redwood and mixed evergreen forest.

locations.name: Dawn Falls
locations.description: A 30-foot waterfall in Baltimore Canyon in Larkspur, tucked into a magical redwood grove. One of Marin's most beloved short hikes, best visited after winter rains.

locations.name: Bridalveil Fall
locations.description: A 620-foot waterfall and one of the iconic symbols of Yosemite Valley. The Ahwahneechee called it Pohono, 'spirit of the puffing wind' — the spray blows sideways in the breeze. A short paved trail leads to the base.

locations.name: Chain Cascade
locations.description: A 1,150-foot waterfall cascading down the Yosemite Valley rim, one of the many spectacular rim falls that appear after winter snowmelt. Visible from the valley floor.

locations.name: Chilnualna Falls
locations.description: A 690-foot multi-tiered waterfall near Wawona in Yosemite, reached via a challenging 8-mile round-trip hike. Less crowded than the valley falls and one of the finest waterfall hikes in the park.
locations.location: Wawona area, Yosemite National Park, Mariposa County

locations.name: Horsetail Fall
locations.description: A 2,130-foot waterfall on El Capitan, famous for the 'Firefall' phenomenon — for a few evenings each February, sunlight illuminates the fall to glow brilliant orange like flowing lava.
locations.location: El Capitan, Yosemite National Park, Mariposa County

locations.name: Illilouette Fall
locations.description: A 370-foot waterfall on Illilouette Creek, best viewed from the Panorama Trail in Yosemite. One of the valley's overlooked gems — farther from the main trails, it rewards those who seek it.
locations.location: Panorama Trail, Yosemite National Park, Mariposa County

locations.name: Lehamite Falls
locations.description: A 1,180-foot waterfall in Indian Canyon on Yosemite's north rim, one of the valley's tallest falls. Flows seasonally after snowmelt and winter rains.
locations.location: Indian Canyon, Yosemite National Park, Mariposa County

locations.name: Nevada Fall
locations.description: A magnificent 594-foot waterfall on the Merced River, reached via the famous Mist Trail in Yosemite. Paired with Vernal Fall below, the hike is among the most spectacular in the park.
locations.location: Mist Trail, Yosemite National Park, Mariposa County

locations.name: Pywiack Cascade
locations.description: A 600-foot cascade on Tenaya Creek in Yosemite. The smooth granite slabs and tiered pools make for spectacular scrambling in the Tenaya Canyon area.
locations.location: Tenaya Creek, Yosemite National Park, Mariposa County

locations.name: Quaking Aspen Falls
locations.description: A 25-foot waterfall in Yosemite's backcountry, surrounded by quaking aspen groves. A quieter, off-the-beaten-path waterfall experience in the park.
locations.location: Yosemite National Park backcountry, Mariposa County

locations.name: Ribbon Fall
locations.description: At 1,612 feet, Ribbon Fall is the highest single-drop waterfall in North America — but only runs in spring. It flows from a hanging valley near El Capitan and dries completely by summer.
locations.location: El Capitan area, Yosemite National Park, Mariposa County

locations.name: Royal Arch Cascade
locations.description: A 1,250-foot seasonal cascade below Royal Arches on Yosemite's north wall, flowing dramatically after winter snows. Best viewed from the valley floor near Ahwahnee Meadow.
locations.location: Royal Arches, Yosemite National Park, Mariposa County

locations.name: Sentinel Fall
locations.description: A 1,920-foot seasonal waterfall below Sentinel Dome on Yosemite Valley's south wall. One of the tallest in Yosemite, it flows in spring and can be seen from multiple points in the valley.

locations.name: Silver Strand Falls
locations.description: A 574-foot waterfall on the south wall of Yosemite Valley, visible from the valley floor near the Pohono Bridge. Its thin silver thread gives it an ethereal quality during spring runoff.

locations.name: Snow Creek Falls
locations.description: A 2,140-foot waterfall above Mirror Lake in Yosemite, one of the tallest in the park. Flows vigorously in spring from Snow Creek Canyon, dropping in multiple tiers to the valley.
locations.location: Mirror Lake area, Yosemite National Park, Mariposa County

locations.name: Staircase Falls
locations.description: A 1,300-foot staircase-like cascade on Yosemite Valley's south wall, flowing in long steps over the granite face. Best seen in early spring from Curry Village.

locations.name: The Cascades
locations.description: A 750-foot waterfall on Cascade Creek in Yosemite, visible from Hwy 140 near the park's western entrance. One of the first waterfalls visitors see arriving via the El Portal corridor.
locations.location: Cascade Creek, Yosemite National Park, Mariposa County

locations.name: Three Chute Falls
locations.description: An 80-foot waterfall in Yosemite's Tenaya Canyon area, where the creek splits into three distinct chutes over polished granite. Remote and seldom visited.
locations.location: Tenaya Canyon, Yosemite National Park, Mariposa County

locations.name: Vernal Fall
locations.description: A stunning 317-foot waterfall on the Mist Trail in Yosemite, where the Merced River thunders over a sheer granite cliff. The trail to the top is one of the most popular in the park — expect to get soaked in spring.
locations.location: Mist Trail, Yosemite National Park, Mariposa County

locations.name: Widow's Tears
locations.description: A 1,170-foot seasonal waterfall on Yosemite Valley's south wall. Flows powerfully in early spring then fades by summer.

locations.name: Wildcat Falls
locations.description: A 720-foot waterfall near The Cascades and Hwy 140 at Yosemite's west entrance, tumbling from a high hanging valley. A dramatic sight for visitors arriving through El Portal.
locations.location: Near El Portal, Yosemite National Park, Mariposa County

locations.name: Yosemite Falls
locations.description: At 2,425 feet, Yosemite Falls is the tallest waterfall in North America. The three-tiered falls are the centerpiece of Yosemite Valley — a short trail reaches the base; a strenuous 7-mile round trip climbs to the top.

locations.name: Leavitt Falls
locations.description: A 200-foot roadside waterfall at Sonora Pass on Hwy 108, one of the most spectacular and accessible waterfalls in the Eastern Sierra. The pullout is right on the highway.

locations.name: Lee Vining Falls
locations.description: A seasonal waterfall tumbling through Lee Vining Canyon on the eastern approach to Tioga Pass. The canyon road offers views of the falls as they rush toward Mono Lake.

locations.name: Minaret Falls
locations.description: A 250-foot cascade in the Ansel Adams Wilderness near Mammoth Lakes, reached by a moderate hike from Agnew Meadows. The jagged Minarets provide a dramatic backdrop.

locations.name: Sardine Falls
locations.description: A scenic cascade near Sonora Pass, reached via an easy 2-mile hike through wildflower meadows. One of the more pleasant hikes in the Sonora Pass area.

locations.name: Limekiln Falls
locations.description: A 100-foot waterfall in Limekiln State Park on Big Sur's southern coast, framed by ancient redwoods. The nearby historic lime kilns from the 1880s add a fascinating cultural dimension.

locations.name: McWay Falls
locations.description: An 80-foot tidefall that drops year-round onto a pristine, inaccessible beach at Julia Pfeiffer Burns State Park — one of the most iconic images of Big Sur. Viewed from an overlook just a short walk from the parking area.
locations.location: Julia Pfeiffer Burns State Park, Monterey County

locations.name: Salmon Creek Falls
locations.description: A 120-foot cascade on Salmon Creek in the southern Big Sur region, accessible via the Salmon Creek Trail in Los Padres National Forest. A lush riparian canyon rewards the moderate hike.
locations.location: Southern Big Sur, Los Padres National Forest, Monterey County

locations.name: Peters Creek Falls
locations.description: A seasonal cascade along Peters Creek in the La Honda area. Part of Pescadero Creek County Park, the falls flow through second-growth redwood forest.
locations.location: Pescadero Creek County Park, San Mateo County

locations.name: Pomponio Falls
locations.description: A 30-foot cascade on Pomponio Creek near La Honda in the Santa Cruz Mountains. Best accessed via trail and flowing strongest in winter and early spring.
locations.location: Pomponio Creek area, San Mateo County

locations.name: Basin Falls
locations.description: A 20-foot waterfall in Uvas Canyon County Park, part of an impressive cluster of falls within a single loop trail. A hidden gem in the Santa Cruz Mountains foothills.
locations.location: Uvas Canyon County Park, Santa Clara County

locations.name: Black Rock Falls
locations.description: A 30-foot waterfall in Uvas Canyon County Park, one of several falls on the Waterfall Loop Trail. The dark volcanic rock gives this cascade its dramatic character.
locations.location: Uvas Canyon County Park, Santa Clara County

locations.name: Granuja Falls
locations.description: A seasonal waterfall in Uvas Canyon County Park on the Waterfall Loop Trail. The name is Spanish for 'rascal' — fitting for a falls that hides in the canyon.
locations.location: Uvas Canyon County Park, Santa Clara County

locations.name: Triple Falls
locations.description: A 35-foot three-tiered waterfall in Uvas Canyon County Park, one of the most photogenic in the South Bay. Part of the Waterfall Loop Trail.
locations.location: Uvas Canyon County Park, Santa Clara County

locations.name: Uvas Falls
locations.description: A seasonal cascade in Uvas Canyon County Park, named after the Spanish word for grapes — a nod to the vineyards of the surrounding valley. Part of the popular Waterfall Loop Trail.
locations.location: Uvas Canyon County Park, Santa Clara County

locations.name: Berry Creek Falls
locations.description: A 65-foot waterfall deep in Big Basin Redwoods State Park, reached via the Skyline-to-Sea Trail. Surrounded by old-growth coast redwoods — one of the finest waterfall hikes in the Santa Cruz Mountains.
locations.location: Big Basin Redwoods State Park, Santa Cruz County

locations.name: Castle Rock Falls
locations.description: An 80-foot seasonal waterfall in Castle Rock State Park on Saratoga Gap, pouring over a sandstone shelf into a boulder-filled pool. Part of a fine loop trail through the park's characteristic tafoni sandstone formations.
locations.location: Castle Rock State Park, Santa Cruz County

locations.name: Golden Cascade
locations.description: A seasonal cascade near Berry Creek Falls in Big Basin Redwoods State Park, glowing amber in the filtered redwood light. Part of the same trail system as Berry Creek Falls.
locations.location: Big Basin Redwoods State Park, Santa Cruz County

locations.name: Silver Falls
locations.description: A 50-foot cascade near Berry Creek Falls in Big Basin Redwoods State Park, often visited together with Golden Cascade on the same loop through old-growth redwood forest.
locations.location: Big Basin Redwoods State Park, Santa Cruz County

locations.name: Marble Falls
locations.description: A 600-foot cascade on the Marble Fork of the Kaweah River in Sequoia National Park, reached via a 7-mile round-trip hike through chaparral and oak woodland. The polished marble rock gives the falls their brilliant white appearance.

locations.name: Tokopah Falls
locations.description: A 1,200-foot cascade at the head of Tokopah Valley in Sequoia National Park, reached via a gentle 4-mile round-trip hike along the Marble Fork Kaweah River. A stunning display of snowmelt in late spring.
locations.location: Tokopah Valley, Sequoia National Park, Tulare County

locations.name: California Falls
locations.description: A 120-foot waterfall in the Grand Canyon of the Tuolumne, reached via the Glen Aulin High Sierra Camp trail in Yosemite. Part of the spectacular series of falls along the Tuolumne River's descent.
locations.location: Grand Canyon of the Tuolumne, Yosemite National Park, Tuolumne County

locations.name: Clavey Falls
locations.description: A dramatic waterfall on the Clavey River, a wild unimpounded tributary of the Tuolumne. A highlight of the kayaking and whitewater community in the Sierra foothills.

locations.name: Le Conte Falls
locations.description: A 250-foot cascade on the Tuolumne River in the Grand Canyon of the Tuolumne, named after pioneering geologist Joseph LeConte. Accessed via the long Glen Aulin trail from Tuolumne Meadows.
locations.location: Grand Canyon of the Tuolumne, Yosemite National Park, Tuolumne County

locations.name: Rancheria Falls
locations.description: A multi-tiered cascade in the Hetch Hetchy area of Yosemite, reached via a 13-mile round-trip trail from O'Shaughnessy Dam that passes Wapama and Tueeulala falls.
locations.location: Hetch Hetchy, Yosemite National Park, Tuolumne County

locations.name: Tueeulala Falls
locations.description: An 880-foot seasonal waterfall at Hetch Hetchy in Yosemite, a slender ribbon of water dropping from the rim above the reservoir. Its delicate flow contrasts with the powerful Wapama Falls nearby.
locations.location: Hetch Hetchy, Yosemite National Park, Tuolumne County

locations.name: Tuolumne Falls
locations.description: A 100-foot cascade on the Tuolumne River near Glen Aulin High Sierra Camp in Yosemite. Part of a remarkable concentration of falls along the Tuolumne's descent from the high country.
locations.location: Glen Aulin area, Yosemite National Park, Tuolumne County

locations.name: Wapama Falls
locations.description: A powerful 1,080-foot waterfall at Hetch Hetchy in Yosemite, so forceful in spring that the trail bridges can be impassable at full flood. A 5-mile round trip from O'Shaughnessy Dam.
locations.location: Hetch Hetchy, Yosemite National Park, Tuolumne County

locations.name: Waterwheel Falls
locations.description: A 360-foot series of falls on the Tuolumne River named for jets of water that arch like spinning wheels during high water. A 16-mile round trip from Tuolumne Meadows — one of Yosemite's most remote waterfall hikes.
locations.location: Tuolumne River, Yosemite National Park, Tuolumne County

locations.name: White Cascade
locations.description: A 75-foot cascade on the Tuolumne River in the Grand Canyon of the Tuolumne, just below Glen Aulin High Sierra Camp. The frothy white water over polished granite gives this cascade its name.
locations.location: Grand Canyon of the Tuolumne, Yosemite National Park, Tuolumne County

locations.name: Bailey Canyon Falls
locations.description: A 20-foot waterfall in Bailey Canyon Wilderness Park in Sierra Madre, reached via a pleasant 3-mile round trip through coastal sage scrub and oak woodland.
locations.location: Bailey Canyon Wilderness Park, Sierra Madre, Los Angeles County

locations.name: Eaton Canyon Falls
locations.description: A 40-foot waterfall in Eaton Canyon Natural Area near Pasadena, one of the most popular waterfall hikes in the Los Angeles area. The 3.5-mile round trip features a fun creek crossing and ends at a shaded plunge pool.
locations.location: Eaton Canyon Natural Area, Pasadena, Los Angeles County

locations.name: Escondido Falls
locations.description: A 200-foot two-tiered waterfall in the Santa Monica Mountains near Malibu, the tallest in the range. A 4-mile round trip through a public easement leads to the lower falls.

locations.name: Falls Creek Falls
locations.description: A 200-foot waterfall in the San Gabriel Mountains of Angeles National Forest. A moderately long hike leads to these falls in a less-traveled canyon.
locations.location: Angeles National Forest, Los Angeles County

locations.name: Fish Canyon Falls
locations.description: An 80-foot three-tiered waterfall near Azusa, once described as the Yosemite of Southern California. Trail access was restored after decades of closure through an adjacent quarry.
locations.location: Fish Canyon, Angeles National Forest, Los Angeles County

locations.name: Hermit Falls
locations.description: A 20-foot waterfall near Chantry Flat in the San Gabriel Mountains, with a deep swimming hole at the base. A popular spot year-round — the trail crosses Arcadia Wilderness Park.
locations.location: Chantry Flat, Arcadia, Los Angeles County

locations.name: Millard Canyon Falls
locations.description: A 60-foot waterfall in Millard Canyon near Altadena, one of the most accessible waterfall hikes in the San Gabriel Mountains. The trail follows the canyon creek through alder groves.
locations.location: Millard Canyon, Altadena, Los Angeles County

locations.name: Newton Canyon Falls
locations.description: A seasonal waterfall in Newton Canyon in the Santa Monica Mountains near Malibu, running through the Zuma/Trancas Canyons Natural Area.
locations.location: Newton Canyon, Malibu, Los Angeles County

locations.name: San Antonio Falls
locations.description: A 75-foot roadside waterfall on Mount Baldy in the San Gabriel Mountains. One of the most accessible waterfalls in Southern California — a short walk from the ski lift parking area.

locations.name: Sturtevant Falls
locations.description: A 60-foot waterfall in Big Santa Anita Canyon near Sierra Madre, one of the most beloved waterfall hikes in Southern California. The 3.5-mile round trip from Chantry Flat winds through a lush alder canyon.
locations.location: Big Santa Anita Canyon, Sierra Madre, Los Angeles County

locations.name: Switzer Falls
locations.description: A 50-foot waterfall in Arroyo Seco canyon off Angeles Crest Highway, site of the historic Switzer's Camp from the 1880s. The 4-mile round trip passes the old resort ruins before reaching the falls.
locations.location: Arroyo Seco, Angeles National Forest, Los Angeles County

locations.name: Trail Canyon Falls
locations.description: A 50-foot waterfall deep in Big Tujunga Canyon in the San Gabriel Mountains, reached via a 5-mile round trip with numerous creek crossings. Remote and rewarding.
locations.location: Big Tujunga Canyon, Angeles National Forest, Los Angeles County

locations.name: Zuma Canyon Falls
locations.description: A 30-foot seasonal waterfall at the head of Zuma Canyon in Malibu. The undeveloped canyon is one of the deepest and wildest in the Santa Monica Mountains.
locations.location: Zuma Canyon, Malibu, Los Angeles County

locations.name: Black Star Canyon Falls
locations.description: A 50-foot seasonal waterfall in Black Star Canyon in the Santa Ana Mountains. The canyon is infamous for its lore and legends — the hike passes through a public easement to reach the falls.
locations.location: Black Star Canyon, Silverado, Orange County

locations.name: Holy Jim Falls
locations.description: A 15-foot waterfall at the end of the popular Holy Jim Trail in the Santa Ana Mountains. A favorite family hike in Orange County, the trail follows Holy Jim Creek through a shaded canyon.

locations.name: Ortega Falls
locations.description: A 35-foot cascade right along Ortega Highway (Hwy 74) in the Santa Ana Mountains, one of the easiest waterfalls to reach in Southern California — the parking area is right on the highway.
locations.location: Ortega Highway, Lake Elsinore, Orange County

locations.name: San Juan Falls
locations.description: A 15-foot seasonal waterfall on San Juan Creek in the Santa Ana Mountains. Part of the popular San Juan Loop Trail in Cleveland National Forest.
locations.location: San Juan Creek, Cleveland National Forest, Orange County

locations.name: Tahquitz Falls
locations.description: A 60-foot waterfall above Palm Springs in Tahquitz Canyon, a site sacred to the Agua Caliente Band of Cahuilla Indians. The tribe operates the canyon as a cultural preserve with an entrance fee.
locations.location: Tahquitz Canyon, Palm Springs, Riverside County

locations.name: Tenaja Falls
locations.description: A spectacular 150-foot five-tiered waterfall in Cleveland National Forest, one of the finest in Southern California. The 1.5-mile trail offers views of multiple tiers cascading over smooth granite.

locations.name: Big Falls
locations.description: At 500 feet, Big Falls in Forest Falls is the tallest waterfall in San Bernardino County and one of the largest in Southern California. A short walk from the parking area leads to a spectacular viewpoint.

locations.name: Bonita Falls
locations.description: A 230-foot seasonal cascade in Lytle Creek Canyon in the San Gabriel Mountains. A short but rugged hike over a dry wash leads to the base of these impressive falls.

locations.name: Etiwanda Falls
